title: Door
medium: Wood, gessoed and lacquered with polychrome decoration and gilding
reveal_text:
First sparked by the accounts of Marco Polo’s 13th-century voyage to China, the Italian vogue for chinoiserie (Chinese-inspired motifs) was later reinforced by the Venetian Republic’s extensive trade with the East. The city-state’s aristocrats ordered entire rooms to be decorated in Chinese style. One palace on the Grand Canal, the Ca’Rezzonico, now a museum of decorative arts, was lavishly decorated by the Rezzonico family after they purchased it in 1750. In addition to commissioning the painter Giovanni Battista Tiepolo and his son Giandomenico to create frescoes for the ceilings, the Rezzonicos followed contemporary fashion by decorating at least one room with Orientalist motifs.

This door from the palace depicts two scenes that provide glimpses of Western perceptions of the East. In the upper panel, a noble couple meets in front of a palm tree; in the lower panel, a pipe-smoking potentate is being served tea. Both scenes are framed by bright sprays of flowers connected with flowing ribbons. The smooth, many layered surface of the lemon yellow door was achieved through lacquering, a technique borrowed from China but practiced in the West with an oil-based mixture rather than the resin-based one employed by Chinese craftsmen.

title: The Crucifixion
reveal_text: In 1626 the Dominican monastery of San Pablo el Real in Seville, Spain, commissioned the young Francisco de Zurbarán to execute a cycle of paintings including The Crucifixion. This work was installed in a dimly lit space in the monastery and was visible to visitors through a grill. Early commentators remarked on its powerful illusion of three-dimensionality, as though it was a sculpture rather than a painting. The dramatically illuminated figure of Christ, set against a dark, empty background, appears outside of time and place, both idealized in its quiet beauty and humanized by the individualized face and insistent realism.

title: Fragment Depicting a Mounted Rider
medium: Linen and wool, slit and dovetailed tapestry weave with eccentric wefts and supplementary wrapping weft details; edged by plain weave with supplementary wrapping wefts forming uncut loop pile
reveal_text: This fragment of a larger furnishing textile may have covered a bed or a sofa. The purple square, carefully woven with a tapestry technique, features contemporary imagery related to hunting, with a central rider on horseback encircled by medallions of vegetation and animals. The complete piece, comprised largely of loop pile weave, would have functioned as padding to create a soft surface. The inclusion of such a detailed tapestry design demonstrates that even an object made for comfort could also introduce rich and colorful adornment into the home.
credit: Gift of Martin A. Ryerson through the Antiquarian Society

title: Funerary Papyrus of Tayuhenutmut
culture_display: Third Intermediate Period, probably Dynasty 21 (about 1069-945 BCE)
reveal_text:
Potent symbols, sacred words, and divine protection helped ancient Egyptians transition from the earthly realm to an eternal existence. Interactions between mortals and the gods were depicted on funerary objects including papyri, like this example made for temple singer Tayu-henut-Mut (“the mistress of [the goddess] Mut”).

This papyrus shows Tayu-henut-Mut standing with raised hands before an offering table, praising the enthroned god Osiris, ruler of the afterlife. Behind Osiris a scribe has carefully copied spells from the Book of Going Forth by Day. First appearing during the New Kingdom (about 1550 BCE), this religious text is better known today as the Book of the Dead, because scrolls were buried with their owners. Tayu-henut-Mut’s copy includes five spells selected from nearly 200 known options to aid in her journey to eternity, including one for “causing a man to remember his name.” Egyptians believed that one would cease to exist in the afterlife if their name was forgotten or no longer spoken among the living.
credit: Gift of Henry H. Getty, Charles L. Hutchinson, Robert H. Fleming, and Norman W. Harris

title: Tunic Fragment
medium: Cotton and wool (camelid), single interlocking tapestry weave
artist_display:
Wari
Probably central or south coast, Peru
reveal_text:
The Wari Empire rose to power near modern-day Ayacucho during the first millennium. Eventually, it came to control much of highland Peru alongside a related state called Tiwanaku in what is now Bolivia. Waris and Tiwanakus developed a shared corpus of imagery that was most elaborately materialized in fine tunics, which were then worn by and buried with elite men. Wari woven designs have some of the highest thread counts of any handwoven fabric made anywhere in the world.

The designs on Wari tunics are highly abstract. One side of each motif is a zigzag line that turns into a spiral (a common but unclear Andean symbol), while the other half represents a face in profile. The vertically divided circle is the eye. The N-shaped area represents grimacing canine teeth. The band with two or three dots above or below it represents a hat. While the faces are clearer in the tunics to the left and right, in this fragment the eye is upside-down and the mouth is where the nose should be.

Similar to other Wari textiles in the Art Institute's collection (including AIC 1955.1784, 1956.95
), this tunic features motifs comprised of faces and stepped spirals, showing how highly regulated Wari designs were. But the most curious aspect of Wari tunics is how the compositions of the motifs become increasingly compressed on the garments’ sides.

title: Half of a Tabard
medium: Cotton, plain weave, with double warps and single wefts; embellished with feathers (likely from great or snowy egrets, Muscovy ducks,Chilean flamigos, and macaws) knotted and attached with cotton in back stitches
artist_display:
Chimú, Ica, or Chincha (“Provincial Inca”?)
Peru, south coast, Possibly Nazca Valley
reveal_text: This man’s garment was woven like a Chimú textile made on the north coast of Peru, but its feline motifs are similar to those created by Ica or Chincha artists of the south coast. It may have been produced when these cultures were subjugated by the Inca Empire. Unfortunately, few similar brown-and-white feathered tunics are currently known, making comparative studies difficult. The Art Institute of Chicago stewards this one as well as a second example, and the Linden Museum in Stuttgart preserves a third. All three were collected around 1900 by a German doctor living in Lima named Eduard Gaffron.

title: Half of a Tabard
medium: Cotton, plain weave; embellished with feathers (likely from Chilean flamigos, macaws, and Muscovy ducks) knotted and attached with cotton in overcast stitches
artist_display: Peru, Probably central or south coast
reveal_text: The absence of cultural information about this feathered garment (and the other two featherworks in our collection: 1955.1778 and 1955.1783) demonstrates what knowledge was lost because of undocumented excavations—especially those that took place in the late 19th century before modern archaeological practices were established. Early collectors rarely kept as robust records as someone might today. Nonetheless, these miraculously well-preserved feathered garments remain a testament to Indigenous creativity and artistry in the Americas.

title: Half of a Tabard
medium: Cotton, plain weave; embellished with feathers (likely from Chilean flamigos, macaws, and Muscovy ducks) knotted and attached with cotton in overcast stitches
reveal_text: Featherworks are challenging for scholars to interpret because they are rare and so materially different from more common woven textiles made by the same cultures. Motifs have various colors because of the vibrant plumages of the birds and have altered forms because of how the artists sewed the strands of feathers to the ground cloths. This tabard is distinct from more common tunics because it was not originally sewn up the sides. A very similar garment in the collection of the Metropolitan Museum of Art in New York has been scientifically dated to 1298–1397.

artist_display:

reveal_text:
The Wari Empire rose to power near modern-day Ayacucho during the first millennium. Eventually, it came to control much of highland Peru alongside a related state called Tiwanaku in what is now Bolivia. The designs on Wari tunics are highly abstract. One side of each motif is a zigzag line that turns into a spiral (a common but unclear Andean symbol), while the other half represents a face in profile. The vertically divided circle is the eye. title: Nuihaku (Noh Costume)
medium: Silk, warp-float faced 7:1 satin weave; patterned with impressed gold leaf; embroidered with silk and gold-leaf-over-lacquered-paper-strip-wrapped cotton in satin and single satin stitches; laid work and couching; lined with silk, plain weave; dyed with beni (safflower)
reveal_text: Many of the elaborately embroidered round crests (mon) on this costume are similar to known personal crests of real actors, but others are imaginary designs. Collectively, they are meant to give this garment graphic impact when worn onstage. In Noh dramas, actors wear stylized masks and use methodical movements to portray narratives based on classical literature or Buddhist beliefs.
credit: Purchased with funds provided by Mrs. Clyde M. Carr

title: Altar Frontal
medium: Silk and gilt-metal-strip-wrapped silk, 4:1 satin damask weave with supplementary brocading wefts bound by main warps in weft-float faced 1:4 's' interlacing; edged with gilt-metal-strip-wrapped silk, plain weave with supplementary patterning warps and extended ground weft uncut fringe and silk, gilt-metal-strip and gilt-metal-strip-wrapped silk, stripes of plain weave with supplementary patterning warps and of 2:2 'z' twill weave with extended ground weft, wrapped, knotted uncut fringe; lining: silk, plain weave
reveal_text: Expensive silk and gold textiles featured prominently in the decoration used in European Catholic churches. Because of their cost, precious materials were often recycled and refashioned. This 16th-century textile altar frontal has been augmented with more recent fringe and trimmings around the perimeter. The placement of these new elements echo the style of 16th-century frontals; they may have replaced originals that had become unsightly with age.
credit: Gift of Edward Morris in memory of his wife

title: Water Lily Pond
reveal_text: In 1893, Claude Monet began transforming the marshy ground behind his Giverny home into a pond, on the narrow end of which he built a Japanese-style wood bridge. Adding both exotic and domestic plantings, including his famous water lilies, the artist created the garden that would be one of his principal subjects for the rest of his life.
credit: Mr. and Mrs. Lewis Larned Coburn Memorial Collection

title: Evening Snow on a Floss Shaper (Nurioke no bosetsu), from the series "Eight Views of the Parlor (Zashiki hakkei)"
artist_display:
Suzuki Harunobu 鈴木 春信 
Japanese, 1725 (?)-1770
reveal_text:
The famous series Eight Views of the Parlor was produced in 1766, around the time  of the emergence of full-color prints in Japan. The series contains eight prints that were housed together in a fanciful wrapper, all of which are now in the Art Institute’s collection. These scenes present visual puns based on well-known themes of the Xiao and Xiang Rivers in China, each of which has its own poetic title. For Evening Snow, Suzuki Harunobu depicted silk floss as snow and the floss shaper as the mountain rather than showing snow-covered hills.

Such witty visual riddles would have been appreciated by the audience of these prints—wealthy, well-educated townsmen who participated in poetry circles. One such figure was Ōkubo Jinshirō Tadanobu, whose pseudonym was Kyosen. It is thought that he produced this set, engaging Harunobu’s services as well as those of the printer. In fact, this image contains Kyosen’s handwritten signature, leading scholars to believe that the Art Institute’s set is the first edition. Sets with Harunobu’s signature exist in other collections.

title: Loop Suspension Bell (Niuzhong)
culture_display: Eastern Zhou dynasty, Spring and Autumn period (770–481 B.C.), 8th/6th century B.C.
reveal_text: Several types of chime bells were cast toward the end of China's Bronze Age and were probably played together in concert. Smallest among these were niuzhong, which had concave rims. Niuzhong were cast in graduated sizes, assembled as a set (often of nine), and suspended from a horizontal beam in order of size. Each bell was designed to emit two distinct tones: one from striking the center of the lower panel, another from striking either corner. The mask centered on the striking panel of this bell is an abstraction of ferocious monster designs seen on earlier bronze vessels.

title: The Holy Family with Saint John the Baptist and Saint Margaret
reveal_text: Ultramarine, the blue pigment used lavishly across this tondo (circular painting), was many times more expensive than gold in the Renaissance.
credit: The Holy Family with Saint John the Baptist and Saint Margaret, c. 1488–93. Filippino Lippi (Italian, c. 1457–1504). Tempera and oil on wood; framed: 184 x 186 x 9.5 cm (72 7/16 x 73 1/4 x 3 3/4 in.); diameter: 153 cm (60 1/4 in.)

title: Horse Stable
medium: Pair of six-panel folding screens; ink, color, and gold on paper
reveal_text: This is the earliest surviving example of the "horse stable" theme in Japanese screens.
credit: Horse Stable (厩図屏風), early 1500s. Japan, Muromachi period (1392–1573). Pair of six-panel folding screens; ink, color, and gold on paper; painting: 146 x 349.6 cm (57 1/2 x 137 5/8 in.)

title: The Sacrifice of Isaac
reveal_text: The angel has two visible sets of legs, showing the artist’s revision of the composition.
credit: The Sacrifice of Isaac, c. 1527. Andrea del Sarto (Italian, 1486–1530). Oil on wood; framed: 208 x 171 x 12.5 cm (81 7/8 x 67 5/16 x 4 15/16 in.); unframed: 178 x 138 cm (70 1/16 x 54 5/16 in.)

title: A Young Woman Buying a Pink from a Young Man
medium: black crayon (wetted and rubbed) heightened with white chalk on faded blue paper
reveal_text: The flower the woman in this drawing plucks from the bouquet—a “pink”—was associated with marriage and love in 18th-century Europe.
credit: A Young Woman Buying a Pink from a Young Man, c. 1740. Giovanni Battista Piazzetta (Italian, 1682–1754). Black crayon (wetted and rubbed) heightened with white chalk on faded blue paper; sheet: 42.7 x 54.9 cm (16 13/16 x 21 5/8 in.)

title: Medicine Master Buddha and the Twelve Divine Generals
medium: Hanging scroll painting; ink, color, gold, and silver on silk
reveal_text: Each of the Divine Generals has an identifying zodiac animal in his hair or headdress.
credit: Medicine Master Buddha and the Twelve Divine Generals (薬師如来及び十二神将像), 1200s. Japan, Kamakura period (1185–1333). Hanging scroll painting; ink, color, gold, and silver on silk; image: 151.2 x 84.1 cm (59 1/2 x 33 1/8 in.); overall: 261 x 106.6 cm (102 3/4 x 41 15/16 in.); with knobs: 261 x 113.3 cm (102 3/4 x 44 5/8 in.)

title: Plaque
culture_display: Peru, North Coast, Chongoyape(?), Chavín style (900-200 BCE)
reveal_text: Hammered gold ornaments have been produced in the Andes since 1500 BCE.
credit: Plaque, c. 500–200 BCE. Peru, North Coast, Chongoyape(?), Chavín style (900-200 BCE). Hammered and cut gold; overall: 12.5 x 13.8 cm (4 15/16 x 5 7/16 in.)

title: Smelting Works at Denver
reveal_text: Moran sketched this image while waiting for his travel arrangements to be resolved, having learned that he received a commission for a large painting of Wyoming for the World’s Columbian Exposition in Chicago in 1893.
credit: Smelting Works at Denver, 1892. Thomas Moran (American, born England, 1837–1926). Watercolor and gouache; sheet: 24 x 31.8 cm (9 7/16 x 12 1/2 in.)

title: Ancestral Commemorative Head (uhunmwun-elao)
culture_display: Nigeria, Benin Kingdom, Ẹdo peoples, members of the Igun Eronmwon (royal brasscasters) guild
reveal_text: The obas (kings) of the Benin Kingdom still wear coral headpieces and jewelry like this sculpted head depicts.
credit: Ancestral Commemorative Head (uhunmwun-elao), possibly mid-1500s or early 1600s. Nigeria, Benin Kingdom, Ẹdo peoples, members of the Igun Eronmwon (royal brasscasters) guild. Copper alloy and iron; overall: 29.9 x 21.6 x 20.4 cm (11 3/4 x 8 1/2 x 8 1/16 in.)

title: Cranes and Serpents
culture_display: China, reportedly from Hunan province, Changsha, Warring States period (475–221 BCE), State of Chu (c. 1046–223 BCE)
reveal_text: The cranes' long necks allowed a lightweight drum to be suspended from strings through the beaks, while the densely intertwined snakes provided a weighty base to stabilize the object during musical performances.
credit: Cranes and Serpents (蛇座鳳鳥鼓架), 475–221 BCE. China, reportedly from Hunan province, Changsha, Warring States period (475–221 BCE), State of Chu (c. 1046–223 BCE). Lacquered wood with polychromy; overall: 132.1 x 124.5 cm (52 x 49 in.)

title: Study for the Nude Youth over the Prophet Daniel (recto); Figure Studies for the Sistine Ceiling (verso)
medium: red chalk over black chalk or charcoal
reveal_text: When Michelangelo ran out of room for the figure's left foot, he turned the paper over and drew the foot in detail along with three additional sketches of the big toe.
credit: Study for the Nude Youth over the Prophet Daniel (recto); Figure Studies for the Sistine Ceiling (verso), 1510–11. Michelangelo Buonarroti (Italian, 1475–1564). Red chalk over black chalk or charcoal; sheet: 34.3 x 24.3 cm (13 1/2 x 9 9/16 in.); secondary support: 34.4 x 24.4 cm (13 9/16 x 9 5/8 in.)

title: Cloth with Procession of Figures
medium: cotton and pigment (field); camelid fiber (borders and fringe)
culture_display: Central Andes, South Coast, Nasca people
reveal_text: Characteristics of the pampas cat and the emperor tamarin monkey are visible in the marching figures.
credit: Cloth with Procession of Figures, 170 BCE–70 CE (radiocarbon date, 95% probability). Central Andes, South Coast, Nasca people. Cotton and pigment (field); camelid fiber (borders and fringe); overall: 69.8 x 280.7 cm (27 1/2 x 110 1/2 in.); mounted: 78.7 x 290.2 cm (31 x 114 1/4 in.)

title: In the Omnibus
reveal_text: The artist Camille Pissarro described the series of prints to which this work belongs as "admirable, as beautiful as Japanese work," praising Cassatt's translation of Ukiyo-e woodblocks.
credit: In the Omnibus, 1890–91. Mary Cassatt (American, 1844–1926). Color drypoint and aquatint; platemark: 36.6 x 26.8 cm (14 7/16 x 10 9/16 in.)

title: Romaine Lacaux
reveal_text: The son of a poor tailor from Limoges, Renoir began his artistic career at age 13 as an apprentice to a porcelain painter.
credit: Romaine Lacaux, 1864. Pierre-Auguste Renoir (French, 1841–1919). Oil on fabric; framed: 106.7 x 89.2 x 8.9 cm (42 x 35 1/8 x 3 1/2 in.); unframed: 81.3 x 65 cm (32 x 25 9/16 in.)

title: The Burning of the Houses of Lords and Commons, 16 October 1834
reveal_text: Turner painted two versions of this event. Cleveland's painting views the fire downriver, from the southeast bank of the Thames, while the second version at the Philadelphia Museum of Art views the fire from directly across Westminster Bridge.
credit: The Burning of the Houses of Lords and Commons, 16 October 1834, 1835. Joseph Mallord William Turner (British, 1775–1851). Oil on canvas; framed: 123.5 x 153.5 x 12 cm (48 5/8 x 60 7/16 x 4 3/4 in.); unframed: 92 x 123.2 cm (36 1/4 x 48 1/2 in.)

title: Saluting Protective Spirit
culture_display: Neo-Assyrian (911–609 BCE), Iraq, Nimrud, Northwest Palace, reign of Ashurnasirpal II (883–859 BCE)
reveal_text: The details of his garments are painstakingly rendered; he wears a long fringed cloak with tassels over a shorter kilt and an under tunic.
credit: Saluting Protective Spirit, 883–859 BCE. Neo-Assyrian (911–609 BCE), Iraq, Nimrud, Northwest Palace, reign of Ashurnasirpal II (883–859 BCE). Gypsum; overall: 229.9 x 137 cm (90 1/2 x 53 15/16 in.)
department: Egyptian and Ancient Near Eastern Art

title: Invocation to Love
medium: Brush and brown wash with graphite squaring lines and underdrawing on cream laid paper
reveal_text: This drawing contains squaring—a grid underlying the image—suggesting it's a smaller drawn replica of a related oil painting.
credit: Invocation to Love, c. 1781. Jean-Honoré Fragonard (French, 1732–1806). Brush and brown wash with graphite squaring lines and underdrawing on cream laid paper; sheet: 33.5 x 41.6 cm (13 3/16 x 16 3/8 in.); secondary support: 37.6 x 47.8 cm (14 13/16 x 18 13/16 in.)

title: Bahram Gur Arrives at the House of a Merchant, text page (recto); Bahram Gur Slays a Dragon (verso), from a Shahnama (Book of Kings) of Firdausi (940–1019 or 1025), known as the Great Mongol Shahnama
medium: opaque watercolor, ink, and gold on paper
reveal_text: The Shahnama is an epic poem that traces the fortunes of heroes and kings, of lovers and enemies.
credit: Bahram Gur Arrives at the House of a Merchant, text page (recto); Bahram Gur Slays a Dragon (verso), from a Shahnama (Book of Kings) of Firdausi (940–1019 or 1025), known as the Great Mongol Shahnama, 1330–35. Iran, Tabriz, Ilkhanid period (1256-1353). Opaque watercolor, ink, and gold on paper; sheet: 45.8 x 34.4 cm (18 1/16 x 13 9/16 in.)

title: Amphora Vase
culture_display: China, Jiangxi Province, Jingdezhen, Qing dynasty (1644–1911), Yongzheng mark and period (1723–35)
reveal_text: The shape of the vase derives from Greece and was introduced to China via the Middle East.
credit: Amphora Vase (雍正粉青釉双龙尊), 1723–35. China, Jiangxi Province, Jingdezhen, Qing dynasty (1644–1911), Yongzheng mark and period (1723–35). Porcelain with celadon glaze; overall: 52.1 cm (20 1/2 in.)

title: The Wade Cup with Animated Script
reveal_text: This is known as the Wade Cup, after J. H. Wade who bequeathed the funds for its acquisition
credit: The Wade Cup with Animated Script, 1200–1221. Iran, Seljuq period of Iran (1037–1194). Brass inlaid with silver; overall: 11.5 cm (4 1/2 in.); diameter: 16.1 cm (6 5/16 in.)

title: Dragon in foliage with lion and phoenix heads
medium: Ink, gum tempera, and gold on paper
reveal_text: The lion head and phoenix head are part of the foliage, like fantastical blossoms.
credit: Dragon in foliage with lion and phoenix heads, mid-1500s. Attributed to Sahkulu (Turkish, active 1526–56). Ink, gum tempera, and gold on paper; sheet: 17.3 x 40.2 cm (6 13/16 x 15 13/16 in.)

title: Sleep and Death Cista Handle
reveal_text: These three figures were cast in eight separate pieces, then welded together and attached to the rectangular base.
credit: Sleep and Death Cista Handle, 400–375 BCE. Etruscan. Bronze; with base: 18.5 x 18.3 cm (7 5/16 x 7 3/16 in.); without base: 14 x 17.4 cm (5 1/2 x 6 7/8 in.)

title: Dead Blue Roller
medium: watercolor and gouache with touches of gold
reveal_text: This realistic portrait of a dead bird signals a turn toward objective depictions of nature in the 1500s.
credit: Dead Blue Roller, 1583. Hans Hoffmann (German, 1545/50–1591/92), after Albrecht Dürer (German, 1471–1528). Watercolor and gouache with touches of gold; sheet: 29.2 x 16.9 cm (11 1/2 x 6 5/8 in.); secondary support: 29.2 x 16.9 cm (11 1/2 x 6 5/8 in.)

title: Saint Jerome and the Lion (From the former Church of St. Peter in Erfurt)
reveal_text: In the story Saint Jerome encounters a fearsome lion, here depicted as diminutive, gentle, and submissive.
credit: Saint Jerome and the Lion (From the former Church of St. Peter in Erfurt), c. 1495. Tilman Riemenschneider (German, active Würzburg, c. 1460–1531). Alabaster, traces of polychromy; overall: 37.8 x 28.1 x 15.9 cm (14 7/8 x 11 1/16 x 6 1/4 in.)

title: Set of Four Armchairs and Settee
medium: carved wood, Savonnerie knotted-pile (symmetrical rug knot) upholstery; wool, hemp
artist_display: Royal Savonnerie Manufactory, Chaillot Workshops (French, est. 1627)
reveal_text: The Savonnerie factory can trace its origins to a small carpet studio which set up shop in 1615 south of Paris in a factory that had formerly made soap (savon in French), hence the name Savonnerie.
credit: Set of Four Armchairs and Settee, c. 1715. Royal Savonnerie Manufactory, Chaillot Workshops (French, est. 1627). Carved wood, Savonnerie knotted-pile (symmetrical rug knot) upholstery; wool, hemp; overall: 120 x 201.3 x 55.9 cm (47 1/4 x 79 1/4 x 22 in.)

title: Portrait of Isabella Brant
reveal_text: First, Rubens painted Isabella’s face, then at a later date he added hands.
credit: Portrait of Isabella Brant, c. 1620–25. Peter Paul Rubens (Flemish, 1577–1640). Oil on wood; framed: 83 x 73.5 x 9 cm (32 11/16 x 28 15/16 x 3 9/16 in.); unframed: 53 x 46 cm (20 7/8 x 18 1/8 in.)

title: The Large Plane Trees (Road Menders at Saint-Rémy)
reveal_text: Van Gogh sometimes created what he called "repetitions," in which he painted the same subject and composition again. This painting has a repetition, currently in the Phillips Collection in Washington, DC. Conservation research has shown that the Cleveland painting is the first version.
credit: The Large Plane Trees (Road Menders at Saint-Rémy), 1889. Vincent van Gogh (Dutch, 1853–1890). Oil on fabric; framed: 104.5 x 124.5 x 7.6 cm (41 1/8 x 49 x 3 in.); unframed: 73.4 x 91.8 cm (28 7/8 x 36 1/8 in.)

title: Birds and Flowers
medium: Pair of six-panel folding screens; ink, color, and gold on paper
reveal_text: The inscriptions on these screens are not signatures of the artist, but instead an attribution to the painter Kano Eitoku (1543–1590) by his youngest brother, Kano Naganobu (1577–1654).
credit: Birds and Flowers (花鳥図屏風), late 1500s. Kano Mitsunobu (Japanese, 1565–1608), and Kano Shōei (Japanese, 1519–1592). Pair of six-panel folding screens; ink, color, and gold on paper; image: 155.5 x 340 cm (61 1/4 x 133 7/8 in.)

title: Torso of Amenpayom
culture_display: Egypt, Greco-Roman period (332 BCE–395 CE), Ptolemaic dynasty (305–30 BCE)
reveal_text: On the back Amenpayom is depicted again worshiping the gods of Mendes, Harpocrates, Banebjedet, and Hatmehit.
credit: Torso of Amenpayom, probably 200–100 BCE. Egypt, Greco-Roman period (332 BCE–395 CE), Ptolemaic dynasty (305–30 BCE). Granodiorite; overall: 96.4 x 32.3 x 28.6 cm (37 15/16 x 12 11/16 x 11 1/4 in.)
department: Egyptian and Ancient Near Eastern Art

title: Gigaku Mask of Young Persian Boy (Taikōji)
reveal_text: This is the mask for one of two sons who accompany a widower in ancient dance theater.
credit: Gigaku Mask of Young Persian Boy (Taikōji) (伎楽面・太孤児), 710–94. Japan, Nara period (710–94). Paulownia wood, lacquered and painted; overall: 28 x 21 x 18.5 cm (11 x 8 1/4 x 7 5/16 in.)

title: Head of a Tahitian Woman
medium: graphite with stumping and graphite wash on parchment
reveal_text: In his writings, Gauguin often referred to his drawings as "documents"—records of motifs and ideas he could use later.
credit: Head of a Tahitian Woman, 1891. Paul Gauguin (French, 1848–1903). Graphite with stumping and graphite wash on parchment; sheet: 30.5 x 24.4 cm (12 x 9 5/8 in.)

title: Cabinet
medium: ebony, marquetry in metal and tortoise shell
reveal_text: Originally designed to sit atop an elaborate tall stand, this chest was likely altered in the late 1700s to reduce its scale and height.
credit: Cabinet, c. 1690. André-Charles Boulle (French, 1642–1732). Ebony, marquetry in metal and tortoise shell; overall: 101.3 x 117.6 x 50 cm (39 7/8 x 46 5/16 x 19 11/16 in.)

title: Church Bells Ringing, Rainy Winter Night
medium: watercolor and gouache over graphite on wove paper
reveal_text: Salem's Baptist Church, a central feature of Burchfield's composition, has since been destroyed by fire.
credit: Church Bells Ringing, Rainy Winter Night, December 1917. Charles Burchfield (American, 1893–1967). Watercolor and gouache over graphite on wove paper; sheet: 77.2 x 50 cm (30 3/8 x 19 11/16 in.); secondary support: 77.5 x 50 cm (30 1/2 x 19 11/16 in.)

title: View of Venice
medium: woodcut from six blocks on six sheets of paper
artist_display: Jacopo de' Barbari (Italian, c. 1460/70–c. 1516); Anton Kolb (German, 1474–1566)
reveal_text: Before the conveniences of airplanes, photography, or even a hot air balloon this monumental bird's-eye view map of Venice made in 1500 documents every building, canal, alleyway, and square in the city.
credit: View of Venice, 1500. Jacopo de' Barbari (Italian, c. 1460/70–c. 1516), published by Anton Kolb (German, 1474–1566). Woodcut from six blocks on six sheets of paper; overall: 132.7 x 277.5 cm (52 1/4 x 109 1/4 in.)

title: Portrait of a Man, Possibly Girolamo Rosati
reveal_text: This fashionable man broadcasts his wealth by wearing a fur-lined velvet and satin outfit with expensive gold jewelry.
credit: Portrait of a Man, Possibly Girolamo Rosati, 1533–34. Lorenzo Lotto (Italian, 1480–1556). Oil on canvas; framed: 135.9 x 128 x 8.6 cm (53 1/2 x 50 3/8 x 3 3/8 in.); unframed: 108.2 x 100.5 cm (42 5/8 x 39 9/16 in.)

title: Equestrian Portrait of the Emperor Maximilian
medium: woodcut, in black and white on paper washed blue
reveal_text: Known as "the last knight," Maximilian was an enthusiast of tournaments and hunting; here he wears the type of fluted armor that later would be named for him.
credit: Equestrian Portrait of the Emperor Maximilian, 1508. Hans Burgkmair (German, 1473–1531). Woodcut, in black and white on paper washed blue; sheet: 32.3 x 22.7 cm (12 11/16 x 8 15/16 in.)

title: Buddha of the Future (Miroku Bosatsu)
medium: cast bronze, incised, with traces of gilding
reveal_text: The Sanskrit name of this being, Maitreya, derives from the Sanskrit word for friend, mita.
credit: Buddha of the Future (Miroku Bosatsu) (弥勒菩薩像), late 600s. Japan, Asuka period (538–710). Cast bronze, incised, with traces of gilding; overall: 45.8 cm (18 1/16 in.); figure: 39.4 cm (15 1/2 in.)

title: Enclosed Valley
medium: etching on cloth, hand colored with watercolor
reveal_text: Though largely unknown today, Hercules Segers was one of Rembrandt's favorite artists: he owned eight paintings and one printing plate by Seghers.
credit: Enclosed Valley, c. 1623–30. Hercules Seghers (Dutch, 1589/90–c. 1638). Etching on cloth, hand colored with watercolor; platemark: 10.7 x 19 cm (4 3/16 x 7 1/2 in.)

title: Sheet of Studies and Sketches
medium: graphite, pen and dark brown ink, and watercolor on thick ivory wove paper
reveal_text: The date Degas wrote on this drawing is incorrect; he didn't visit Florence until 1858, and must have inscribed the sheet years later.
credit: Sheet of Studies and Sketches, 1858. Edgar Degas (French, 1834–1917). Graphite, pen and dark brown ink, and watercolor on thick ivory wove paper; sheet: 30.4 x 23.5 cm (11 15/16 x 9 1/4 in.)

title: Saint Peter Repentant
reveal_text: The rooster next to Peter is a reference to Jesus's prediction that Peter will betray him, stating: "Before the rooster crows, you will disown me three times."
credit: Saint Peter Repentant, 1645. Georges de La Tour (French, 1593–1652). Oil on canvas; framed: 140.3 x 119.1 x 7 cm (55 1/4 x 46 7/8 x 2 3/4 in.); unframed: 114 x 95 cm (44 7/8 x 37 3/8 in.)

title: Backrest of a Litter
medium: mixed media:  wood, gold alloy, pigment, shell inlay
culture_display: Central Andes, North Coast, Chimú people, late Intermediate period
reveal_text: Cinnabar, a toxic mercuric sulfide, was used to color the red background and faces of the figures.
credit: Backrest of a Litter, 1185–1275 (radiocarbon date, 95.4% probability). Central Andes, North Coast, Chimú people, late Intermediate period. Mixed media:  wood, gold alloy, pigment, shell inlay; overall: 60.4 x 95 cm (23 3/4 x 37 3/8 in.)

title: Swift Bull from the Ten Swift Bulls
medium: section of a handscroll mounted as a hanging scroll; ink and slight color on paper
reveal_text: We know the original scroll had ten bulls because a copy of the complete scroll exists.
credit: Swift Bull from the Ten Swift Bulls (駿牛図　断簡), late 1200s–early 1300s. Japan, Kamakura period (1185–1333). Section of a handscroll mounted as a hanging scroll; ink and slight color on paper; painting: 27.1 x 31.9 cm (10 11/16 x 12 9/16 in.)

title: The Submersion of Pharaoh's Army in the Red Sea
reveal_text: Titian may have identified the Egyptians in this composition, drowned in the Red Sea as they pursue the fleeing Israelites, with the League of Cambrai, a military alliance formed by the major powers in southern Europe and a menace to his hometown of Venice.
credit: The Submersion of Pharaoh's Army in the Red Sea, 1514–15, printed 1549. Titian (Italian, c. 1488–1576), published by Domenico dalle Greche (Italian, 1503–1603). Woodcut; unframed: 40 x 55 cm (15 3/4 x 21 5/8 in.)

title: Head of Amenhotep III Wearing the Blue Crown
culture_display: Egypt, New Kingdom (1540–1069 BCE), Dynasty 18, reign of Amenhotep III (1390–1352 BCE)
reveal_text: The Blue Crown or Khepresh was worn by New Kingdom Pharaohs into battle as well as in ceremonies.
credit: Head of Amenhotep III Wearing the Blue Crown, c. 1391–1353 BCE. Egypt, New Kingdom (1540–1069 BCE), Dynasty 18, reign of Amenhotep III (1390–1352 BCE). Granodiorite; overall: 39.1 x 30.3 x 27.7 cm (15 3/8 x 11 15/16 x 10 7/8 in.)
department: Egyptian and Ancient Near Eastern Art

title: Candelabrum Stand of a Dancing Maenad
reveal_text: A small turtle beneath the figure’s back foot suggests a rustic setting.
credit: Candelabrum Stand of a Dancing Maenad, 525–500 BCE. Etruscan. Bronze; overall: 18.8 cm (7 3/8 in.)

title: Torso of a Kouros
reveal_text: In antiquity, brightly colored paint would probably have rendered this kouros more lifelike.
credit: Torso of a Kouros, c. 550 BCE. Greece, Archaic period. Marble; overall: 62.5 cm (24 5/8 in.)

title: Mandala of the Three Shrines at Kumano
medium: hanging scroll; ink and color on silk
culture_display: Japan, Kamakura period (1185–1333) to Nanbokuchō period (1336–92)
reveal_text: The sacred waterfall at the top right of the painting is called Nachi.
credit: Mandala of the Three Shrines at Kumano (熊野宮曼荼羅図), 1300s. Japan, Kamakura period (1185–1333) to Nanbokuchō period (1336–92). Hanging scroll; ink and color on silk; image: 134 x 62 cm (52 3/4 x 24 7/16 in.); overall: 217.2 x 80 cm (85 1/2 x 31 1/2 in.)

title: The Story of Fukutomi
medium: Handscroll; ink and color on paper
reveal_text: This scroll is treasured as the second oldest surviving version of this story.
credit: The Story of Fukutomi (福富草紙), mid-1400s. Japan, Muromachi period (1392–1573). Handscroll; ink and color on paper; image: 35.3 x 1028.8 cm (13 7/8 x 405 1/16 in.); overall: 37 x 1067 cm (14 9/16 x 420 1/16 in.)

title: Ewer
medium: lead-glazed, white-paste earthenware with inlaid slip decoration
reveal_text: The green tree frog was one of the symbols of French King Henri II, indicating that this ewer may have either been made for the king or one of his courtiers.
credit: Ewer, c. 1540–67. Saint-Porchaire (French). Lead-glazed, white-paste earthenware with inlaid slip decoration; overall: 35.6 x 13.7 cm (14 x 5 3/8 in.)

title: Feast Ladle
medium: horn, bone, copper, abalone shell inlay
culture_display: Native North America, Northwest Coast, Alaska, Tlingit
reveal_text: An imposing eagle presides over the bowl of this impressive ladle.
credit: Feast Ladle, late 1800s–early 1900s. Native North America, Northwest Coast, Alaska, Tlingit. Horn, bone, copper, abalone shell inlay; overall: 13 cm (5 1/8 in.)

title: Boy with Anchor
reveal_text: The mottled texture of the sky is an example of Homer’s blotting technique—a subtractive process in which he applied a wash of water, sprinkled some breadcrumbs, and then gently rubbed the paper with his fingers in order to absorb the extra color, leaving behind a granulated texture.
credit: Boy with Anchor, 1873. Winslow Homer (American, 1836–1910). Watercolor and gouache with graphite; sheet: 19.4 x 34.9 cm (7 5/8 x 13 3/4 in.)

title: Bastille Day
reveal_text: This print is one of the few works that can be dated to Maurice Prendergast's early years in Paris.
credit: Bastille Day, 1892. Maurice Prendergast (American, born Newfoundland [now Canada], 1858–1924). Color monotype; image: 17.4 x 13.1 cm (6 7/8 x 5 3/16 in.); platemark: 25.5 x 20 cm (10 1/16 x 7 7/8 in.); sheet: 30.5 x 24.8 cm (12 x 9 3/4 in.)

title: Claire Campbell
medium: Pastel with oil on fine-weave canvas
reveal_text: Edouard Manet almost always depicted the subjects of his pastel portraits against plain colored backgrounds, as seen in this work.
credit: Claire Campbell, 1876. Édouard Manet (French, 1832–1883). Pastel with oil on fine-weave canvas; unframed: 55.2 x 45.7 cm (21 3/4 x 18 in.)

title: The Illustrated Miraculous Origins of the Yūzū Nenbutsu School
medium: Handscroll; ink, color, and gold on paper
reveal_text: The other scroll from this two-scroll set belongs to the Art Institute of Chicago.
credit: The Illustrated Miraculous Origins of the Yūzū Nenbutsu School (融通念仏縁起絵巻), early 1300s. Japan, Kamakura period (1185–1333). Handscroll; ink, color, and gold on paper; painting: 29.7 x 1232.4 cm (11 11/16 x 485 3/16 in.); mounted: 30.3 x 1373.5 cm (11 15/16 x 540 3/4 in.)

title: Brush Washer
culture_display: China, Henan Province, Baofeng, Qingliangsi, Northern Song dynasty (960-1127)
reveal_text: The little black spot in the glaze inside the dish is one of the slight imperfections characteristic of genuine Ru ware.
credit: Brush Washer (汝瓷筆洗盤), late 1000s–1127. China, Henan Province, Baofeng, Qingliangsi, Northern Song dynasty (960-1127). Porcelaneous stoneware, Ru ware; diameter: 12.8 cm (5 1/16 in.); overall: 3.8 cm (1 1/2 in.)

title: Farmhouse on the Slope of a Hill
reveal_text: The artist who made this drawing, Fra (brother) Bartolommeo, left his successful Florentine artistic studio in 1500 and renounced painting in order to join the Dominican Order.
credit: Farmhouse on the Slope of a Hill, c. 1508. Fra Bartolommeo (Italian, 1472–1517). Pen and brown ink; sheet: 22.3 x 29.4 cm (8 3/4 x 11 9/16 in.); secondary support: 27.5 x 41.2 cm (10 13/16 x 16 1/4 in.)

title: Wine Cup with Children at Play
medium: porcelain with underglaze blue and overglaze enamel decoration, docai ("joined colors") ware
culture_display: China, Jiangxi province, Jingdezhen, Ming dynasty (1368-1644), Chenghua mark and period (1465-1487)
reveal_text: Chenghua ware demonstrates one of the high points of technological accomplishment in Chinese porcelain production.
credit: Wine Cup with Children at Play (斗彩婴戏杯), 1465–87. China, Jiangxi province, Jingdezhen, Ming dynasty (1368-1644), Chenghua mark and period (1465-1487). Porcelain with underglaze blue and overglaze enamel decoration, docai ("joined colors") ware; overall: 4.8 cm (1 7/8 in.)

title: Mount Sainte-Victoire
reveal_text: Cezanne created 36 paintings and 45 watercolors of Mount Sainte-Victoire. He was preoccupied with this subject and painted this mountain repeatedly until his death.
credit: Mount Sainte-Victoire, c. 1904. Paul Cezanne (French, 1839–1906). Oil on fabric; framed: 87.5 x 106.5 x 7 cm (34 7/16 x 41 15/16 x 2 3/4 in.); unframed: 72.2 x 92.4 cm (28 7/16 x 36 3/8 in.)

title: Landscape with Wheelbarrow
medium: watercolor and opaque watercolor with black chalk on cream paper
reveal_text: Vincent van Gogh made this watercolor just over a year after beginning to work professionally as an artist.
credit: Landscape with Wheelbarrow, 1883. Vincent van Gogh (Dutch, 1853–1890). Watercolor and opaque watercolor with black chalk on cream paper; sheet: 24.9 x 35.7 cm (9 13/16 x 14 1/16 in.); framed: 39.8 x 52.4 x 2.6 cm (15 11/16 x 20 5/8 x 1 in.)

title: At the Concert Parisien
medium: conté crayon heightened with white chalk on cream handmade modern laid paper
reveal_text: Despite Georges Seurat's brief lifespan, he created a large number of drawings, working almost exclusively with the waxy Conté crayon and textured paper used for this sheet.
credit: At the Concert Parisien, 1887–88. Georges Seurat (French, 1859–1891). Conté crayon heightened with white chalk on cream handmade modern laid paper; sheet: 31.4 x 23.6 cm (12 3/8 x 9 5/16 in.)

title: The Red Kerchief
reveal_text: Madame Monet’s red cape draws the viewer’s attention beyond the glass of the French doors capturing her glance, a brief moment frozen in time. This painting was very special to Monet and he kept it with him for his entire life.
credit: The Red Kerchief, c. 1868–73. Claude Monet (French, 1840–1926). Oil on fabric; framed: 128.3 x 105.7 x 14.6 cm (50 1/2 x 41 5/8 x 5 3/4 in.); unframed: 99 x 79.8 cm (39 x 31 7/16 in.)

title: Saint Sebastian
reveal_text: The elegant pose of Saint Sebastian here was used by the artist in at least two finished paintings of the saint.
credit: Saint Sebastian, c. 1493. Perugino (Italian, c. 1450/55–1523). Metalpoint; sheet: 25.6 x 14.6 cm (10 1/16 x 5 3/4 in.)

title: Morning Glory with Black
reveal_text: The morning glory rests against a black petunia, seen partially at lower right.
credit: Morning Glory with Black, 1926. Georgia O'Keeffe (American, 1887–1986). Oil on canvas; framed: 96.5 x 81.3 x 5.4 cm (38 x 32 x 2 1/8 in.); unframed: 91 x 75.5 cm (35 13/16 x 29 3/4 in.)

title: Hunting near Hartenfels Castle
medium: oil, originally on wood, transferred to masonite
reveal_text: Humans, dogs, and deer make up most of the creatures in this busy scene—but not all of them! Look closely at the background to spot a bear and three boars.
credit: Hunting near Hartenfels Castle, 1540. Lucas Cranach (German, 1472–1553). Oil, originally on wood, transferred to masonite; framed: 133 x 185.5 x 7.3 cm (52 3/8 x 73 1/16 x 2 7/8 in.); unframed: 116.8 x 170.2 cm (46 x 67 in.)

title: Mourner from the Tomb of Philip the Bold, Duke of Burgundy (1364–1404)
reveal_text: A plaster cast of this mourner decorates the empty niche in the Tomb of Philip the Bold in Dijon, France.
credit: Mourner from the Tomb of Philip the Bold, Duke of Burgundy (1364–1404), 1404–10. Claus de Werve (Netherlandish, 1380–1439). Vizille alabaster; overall: 41 x 12.7 x 15 cm (16 1/8 x 5 x 5 7/8 in.)

title: Dragon and Tiger
medium: Pair of six-panel folding screens; ink on paper
culture_display: Japan, Muromachi period (1392–1573) to Momoyama period (1573–1615)
reveal_text: This pair of screens is considered the masterpiece of Sesson's body of work, and may have been created for the lord of Odawara in eastern Japan.
credit: Dragon and Tiger (龍虎図屏風), c. 1546–56. Sesson Shūkei (Japanese, c. 1492–c. 1577). Pair of six-panel folding screens; ink on paper; each: 157.3 x 339 cm (61 15/16 x 133 7/16 in.); framed: 172.3 x 354 cm (67 13/16 x 139 3/8 in.)

title: Evening, Melancholy I
reveal_text: This print is one of only two known impressions printed from the first state of the block before it was cut into two sections.
credit: Evening, Melancholy I, 1896. Edvard Munch (Norwegian, 1863–1944). Hand-colored woodcut; image: 37.7 x 45 cm (14 13/16 x 17 11/16 in.)

title: Christ and the Virgin in the House at Nazareth
reveal_text: Zurbarán, also a still-life painter, tilts the tabletop to emphasize the symbolic books and fruit.
credit: Christ and the Virgin in the House at Nazareth, c. 1640. Francisco de Zurbarán (Spanish, 1598–1664). Oil on canvas; framed: 201 x 256 x 10 cm (79 1/8 x 100 13/16 x 3 15/16 in.); unframed: 165 x 218.2 cm (64 15/16 x 85 7/8 in.)

title: Statue of Amenemhat III
culture_display: Egypt, Middle Kingdom (2040–1648 BCE), Dynasty 12, reign of Amenemhat III (1860–1814 BCE)
reveal_text: The first pyramid Amenemhat III attempted to build, the so-called Black Pyramid, had major construction flaws and had to be abandoned when it started to collapse.
credit: Statue of Amenemhat III, c. 1859–1814 BCE. Egypt, Middle Kingdom (2040–1648 BCE), Dynasty 12, reign of Amenemhat III (1860–1814 BCE). Granodiorite; overall: 51.2 x 19.8 x 18.4 cm (20 3/16 x 7 13/16 x 7 1/4 in.)
department: Egyptian and Ancient Near Eastern Art

title: Water Lilies (Agapanthus)
reveal_text: Water lilies were a recurring theme in Monet's work; he painted around 250 water lily compositions.
credit: Water Lilies (Agapanthus), c. 1915–26. Claude Monet (French, 1840–1926). Oil on canvas; framed: 204.9 x 430.3 x 6 cm (80 11/16 x 169 7/16 x 2 3/8 in.); unframed: 201.3 x 425.6 cm (79 1/4 x 167 9/16 in.)

title: Nursing Woman with Child
culture_display: Africa, West Africa, Côte d'Ivoire, reportedly Korhogo area, Senufo-style carver
reveal_text: The mother’s face, adorned with incisions and markings, resembles that of women who have reached puberty.
credit: Nursing Woman with Child, 1800s-1900s. Africa, West Africa, Côte d'Ivoire, reportedly Korhogo area, Senufo-style carver. Wood and organic material; overall: 63.6 cm (25 1/16 in.)

title: Nome Gods Bearing Offerings
culture_display: Egypt, New Kingdom (1540–1069 BCE), Dynasty 18, reign of Amenhotep III (1390–1352 BCE)
reveal_text: Another block from the same temple wall was acquired 15 years later by the museum.
credit: Nome Gods Bearing Offerings, c. 1391–1353 BCE. Egypt, New Kingdom (1540–1069 BCE), Dynasty 18, reign of Amenhotep III (1390–1352 BCE). Painted limestone; overall: 66 x 133 cm (26 x 52 3/8 in.)
department: Egyptian and Ancient Near Eastern Art

title: Portrait of Catherine Grey, Lady Manners
reveal_text: Catherine was an Irish poet who wrote of longing to escape the fashionable world.
credit: Portrait of Catherine Grey, Lady Manners, 1794. Thomas Lawrence (British, 1769–1830). Oil on canvas; framed: 280.5 x 185 x 9 cm (110 7/16 x 72 13/16 x 3 9/16 in.); unframed: 255.3 x 158 cm (100 1/2 x 62 3/16 in.)

title: Head Fragment
reveal_text: In ancient Mesoamerica, jade was more valuable than gold.
credit: Head Fragment, c. 900–300 BCE. Mesoamerica, Mexico, Olmec. Jadeite; overall: 7.4 x 6.2 x 5 cm (2 15/16 x 2 7/16 x 1 15/16 in.)

title: Head of Amenhotep III Wearing the Round Wig
culture_display: Egypt, New Kingdom (1540–1069 BCE), Dynasty 18, reign of Amenhotep III (1390–1352 BCE)
reveal_text: Amenhotep wears a chin strap that would have held up a false beard.
credit: Head of Amenhotep III Wearing the Round Wig, c. 1391–1353 BCE. Egypt, New Kingdom (1540–1069 BCE), Dynasty 18, reign of Amenhotep III (1390–1352 BCE). Brown quartzite; overall: 17.3 x 17 x 25.3 cm (6 13/16 x 6 11/16 x 9 15/16 in.)
department: Egyptian and Ancient Near Eastern Art

title: Sun Bodhisattva (Nikkō Bosatsu)
medium: Japanese nutmeg-yew wood with traces of color and gold
reveal_text: Japanese nutmeg-yew, or kaya, was used as a replacement for the unobtainable sandalwood of which small Buddhist sculptures imported from China were made.
credit: Sun Bodhisattva (Nikkō Bosatsu) (日光菩薩像), c. 800. Japan, Heian period (794–1185). Japanese nutmeg-yew wood with traces of color and gold; overall: 46.7 cm (18 3/8 in.)

title: Jar with Lion-Head Handles
culture_display: China, Jiangxi Province, Jingdezhen, Yuan dynasty (1271–1368)
reveal_text: The characters for "palace" and "south" are scratched into the white glaze on the vessel's shoulder, suggesting that it was once kept in a specific location in a palace.
credit: Jar with Lion-Head Handles (青花雙獅頭罐), 1300s. China, Jiangxi Province, Jingdezhen, Yuan dynasty (1271–1368). Porcelain with underglaze painted decoration; diameter of mouth: 15.6 cm (6 1/8 in.); overall: 39.4 x 37.5 cm (15 1/2 x 14 3/4 in.)

title: Panel with Royal Woman
culture_display: Guatemala or Mexico, Usumacinta River region, Maya style
artist_display: K’in Lakam Chahk; Jun Nat Omootz
reveal_text: This panel, likely from a palace interior, depicts a royal Maya woman.
credit: Panel with Royal Woman, c. 795. Sculpted by K’in Lakam Chahk, and Jun Nat Omootz. Limestone; overall: 60.4 x 69.8 cm (23 3/4 x 27 1/2 in.)

title: Cupid and Psyche
reveal_text: Jacques-Louis David included two butterflies in this painting: one above the slumbering Psyche and the other on the base of the couple's bed frame.
credit: Cupid and Psyche, 1817. Jacques-Louis David (French, 1748–1825). Oil on canvas; framed: 221 x 282 x 10 cm (87 x 111 x 3 15/16 in.); unframed: 184.2 x 241.6 cm (72 1/2 x 95 1/8 in.)

title: Satyress and Child
reveal_text: An invention of European artists of the post-Roman period, a satyress is the female equivalent of the male satyr in classical antiquity. Part human and part goat or horse, the satyress can be recognized by her animal legs and hoofs.
credit: Satyress and Child, 1803. Clodion (French, 1738–1814). Terracotta; diameter: 30.7 cm (12 1/16 in.)

title: Mural Fragment with Elite Male and Maguey Cactus Leaves
culture_display: Central Mexico, Tlacuilapaxco apartment compound(?), Teotihuacán style, Classic Period
reveal_text: This fragment is from a larger composition that covered the walls of a patio at Teotihuacan.
credit: Mural Fragment with Elite Male and Maguey Cactus Leaves, 500–550. Central Mexico, Tlacuilapaxco apartment compound(?), Teotihuacán style, Classic Period. Fresco on wall fragment; overall: 83.2 x 116.2 cm (32 3/4 x 45 3/4 in.)

title: Hours of Queen Isabella the Catholic, Queen of Spain
medium: ink, tempera, and gold on vellum
artist_display: Master of the First Prayerbook of Maximillian (Flemish, c. 1444–1519); Associates
reveal_text: Isabella’s book of hours may have in its margins one of the first depictions of a daffodil in manuscript painting.
credit: Hours of Queen Isabella the Catholic, Queen of Spain, c. 1500. Master of the First Prayerbook of Maximillian (Flemish, c. 1444–1519), and Associates. Ink, tempera, and gold on vellum; codex: 23.5 x 17.3 x 4.8 cm (9 1/4 x 6 13/16 x 1 7/8 in.)

title: Cosmetics Box with Chrysanthemums
reveal_text: In Japanese, this type of small box is called a tebako, literally a hand box, and was typically part of a cosmetics set.
credit: Cosmetics Box with Chrysanthemums (菊蒔絵手箱), early 1300s. Japan, Kamakura period (1185–1333). Lacquer on wood; overall: 17.6 x 29 x 22.8 cm (6 15/16 x 11 7/16 x 9 in.)

title: Imperial Red Cross Easter Egg
medium: gold, silver gilt, enamel, glass, ivory
artist_display: Peter Carl Fabergé (Russian, 1846–1920); Henrik Wigström (Russian, 1862–1923); House of Fabergé (Russian, 1842–1918)
reveal_text: This egg was confiscated by the Bolsheviks during the Russian Revolution and sold by the government of Josef Stalin in the 1930s to raise money for the Soviet treasury.
credit: Imperial Red Cross Easter Egg, 1915. Peter Carl Fabergé (Russian, 1846–1920), Henrik Wigström (Russian, 1862–1923), House of Fabergé (Russian, 1842–1918). Gold, silver gilt, enamel, glass, ivory; overall: 8.6 x 6.4 cm (3 3/8 x 2 1/2 in.)

title: La Cervara, the Roman Campagna
reveal_text: In 1845, the French poet Charles Baudelaire proclaimed Corot the leading painter of the modern landscape.
credit: La Cervara, the Roman Campagna, c. 1830–31. Jean Baptiste Camille Corot (French, 1796–1875). Oil on fabric; framed: 130 x 167.5 x 9.5 cm (51 3/16 x 65 15/16 x 3 3/4 in.); unframed: 97.6 x 135.8 cm (38 7/16 x 53 7/16 in.)

title: Paulownias and Chrysanthemums
medium: Two-panel folding screen; ink and color on gilded paper
reveal_text: Hōitsu often painted two-panel folding screens for urban clients residing in smaller spaces. A painting after this one in the Itabashi Museum in Tokyo shows an extended composition across a pair of two-panel screens.
credit: Paulownias and Chrysanthemums (桐菊流水図屏風), early 1800s. Sakai Hōitsu (Japanese, 1761–1828). Two-panel folding screen; ink and color on gilded paper; image: 152.7 x 154.9 cm (60 1/8 x 61 in.); overall: 157.5 x 158.5 cm (62 x 62 3/8 in.)

title: Panoramic View of the Alps, Les Dents du Midi
reveal_text: This late, unfinished painting reveals some of Courbet's techniques. At the top of the mountains you can see how different brushstrokes and thickly applied paint were used to differentiate the texture of the clouds from the snow.
credit: Panoramic View of the Alps, Les Dents du Midi, 1877. Gustave Courbet (French, 1819–1877). Oil on fabric; framed: 172 x 230 x 8.5 cm (67 11/16 x 90 9/16 x 3 3/8 in.); unframed: 151.2 x 210.2 cm (59 1/2 x 82 3/4 in.)

title: Portrait of the Jester Calabazas
reveal_text: Calabazas holds a wind-driven pinwheel and a miniature portrait of a woman, together suggesting the inconstancy of love.
credit: Portrait of the Jester Calabazas, c. 1631–32. Diego Velázquez (Spanish, 1599–1660). Oil on canvas; framed: 199.3 x 133.1 x 12.7 cm (78 7/16 x 52 3/8 x 5 in.); unframed: 175 x 106 cm (68 7/8 x 41 3/4 in.)

title: Twilight in the Wilderness
reveal_text: An image of this painting has a cameo in the Harrison Ford film Clear and Present Danger.
credit: Twilight in the Wilderness, 1860. Frederic Edwin Church (American, 1826–1900). Oil on canvas; unframed: 101.6 x 162.6 cm (40 x 64 in.); framed: 124 x 185 x 13 cm (48 13/16 x 72 13/16 x 5 1/8 in.)

title: Jonah Swallowed
reveal_text: Although usually thought of as a whale, here the artist has depicted Jonah swallowed by a cetus, a large, monstrous sea creature.
credit: Jonah Swallowed, 280–90 CE. Anatolia, late Roman-early Christian. Marble; overall: 50.4 x 15.5 x 26.9 cm (19 13/16 x 6 1/8 x 10 9/16 in.)

title: Arm of Eve
medium: point of brush and gray and black wash, brush and gray and black wash, heightened with white gouache
reveal_text: The blue paper that Dürer used to make this drawing was a specialty of Venetian paper makers in the 16th century, who achieved the color by infusing the paper pulp with dyed fibers.
credit: Arm of Eve, 1507. Albrecht Dürer (German, 1471–1528). Point of brush and gray and black wash, brush and gray and black wash, heightened with white gouache; sheet: 34.4 x 26.7 cm (13 9/16 x 10 1/2 in.)

title: White-Ground Lekythos (Oil Vessel): Atalanta and Erotes
reveal_text: Eros, god of love, appears three times on this vase.
credit: White-Ground Lekythos (Oil Vessel): Atalanta and Erotes, c. 500–490 BCE. Attributed to Douris (Greek, Attic, active c. 500–470 BCE). Ceramic; overall: 31.8 cm (12 1/2 in.)

title: Selections from the New Collection of Japanese Poems from Ancient and Modern Times (Shinkokin wakashū) with Printed Designs of Chinese Lions and Crab Finger Grass
medium: handscroll; ink, gold, and silver on paper
reveal_text: Before becoming a master painter of screens, Tawaraya Sōtatsu painted fans.
credit: Selections from the New Collection of Japanese Poems from Ancient and Modern Times (Shinkokin wakashū) with Printed Designs of Chinese Lions and Crab Finger Grass (目日芝に唐獅子摺絵新古今集和歌巻), early 1600s. Hon'ami Kōetsu (Japanese, 1558–1637), Tawaraya Sōtatsu (Japanese, c. 1570–c. 1640). Handscroll; ink, gold, and silver on paper; overall: 23.2 x 346.6 cm (9 1/8 x 136 7/16 in.)

title: Estérel Village
reveal_text: Edgar Degas made a total of about 60 similarly abstract landscapes using monotype around the time he made this print.
credit: Estérel Village, c. 1890. Edgar Degas (French, 1834–1917). Color monotype; sheet: 29.9 x 39.9 cm (11 3/4 x 15 11/16 in.); image: 29.9 x 39.9 cm (11 3/4 x 15 11/16 in.)

title: St. Catherine of Siena and the Beggar
reveal_text: Giovanni di Paolo painted both large altarpieces and small manuscripts.
credit: St. Catherine of Siena and the Beggar, 1460s. Giovanni di Paolo (Italian, c. 1403–1482). Tempera and gold on wood; framed: 35.6 x 35.7 x 4.5 cm (14 x 14 1/16 x 1 3/4 in.); unframed: 28.7 x 28.9 cm (11 5/16 x 11 3/8 in.)

title: The Vicarello Goblet
culture_display: Italy, Vicarello (ancient Aquae Apollinares), Roman, Augustan period
reveal_text: This silver cup was found north of Rome at Vicarello (ancient Aquae Apollinares), probably in 1862.
credit: The Vicarello Goblet, 25 BCE–25 CE. Italy, Vicarello (ancient Aquae Apollinares), Roman, Augustan period. Silver; overall: 12.2 x 7.8 cm (4 13/16 x 3 1/16 in.)

title: Hinds House Window
artist_display: Tiffany Glass & Decorating Company (America, New York, 1892–1902)
reveal_text: This window incorporates layered pieces of glass, producing shadows and creating a multidimensional quality.
credit: Hinds House Window, c. 1900. Tiffany Glass & Decorating Company (America, New York, 1892–1902). Leaded glass; unframed: 227.3 x 114.3 cm (89 1/2 x 45 in.)

title: Wrestlers in a Circus
reveal_text: During the 1930s, 639 works by Kirchner were removed from German museums and either destroyed or sold to foreign collectors and museums. The year after the Nazis organized the Degenerate Art exhibition in Munich, Kirchner committed suicide.
credit: Wrestlers in a Circus, 1909. Ernst Ludwig Kirchner (German, 1880–1938). Oil on canvas; framed: 107.5 x 121 x 7 cm (42 5/16 x 47 5/8 x 2 3/4 in.); unframed: 80.5 x 94 cm (31 11/16 x 37 in.)

title: Battle of the Nudes
reveal_text: Considered a groundbreaking work in the history of the Italian Renaissance, the CMA holds the only known impression of the print's first state.
credit: Battle of the Nudes, 1470s–80s. Antonio del Pollaiuolo (Italian, 1431/32–1498). Engraving; platemark: 42 x 60.4 cm (16 9/16 x 23 3/4 in.); sheet: 42.4 x 60.9 cm (16 11/16 x 24 in.)

title: Icon of the Virgin and Child
reveal_text: Created in Egypt's hot and dry desert climate, this tapestry features a border of lush greenery and fruits, while flowers peek out from around the throne of the Virgin. Imagine encountering this artistic oasis in a Coptic church during the dry heat of the day.
credit: Icon of the Virgin and Child, 500s CE. Egypt, Byzantine period. Wool and dye; overall: 178.7 x 110.5 cm (70 3/8 x 43 1/2 in.); mounted: 197.4 x 128.2 x 6.4 cm (77 11/16 x 50 1/2 x 2 1/2 in.)

title: Front Face of a Stela (Free-standing Stone with Relief)
culture_display: Mesoamerica, Guatemala, Department of the Petén, El Perú (also known as Waka'), Maya (250-900), Classic period (200-1000)
reveal_text: The powerful Maya queen shown on this stela is known as Lady K’abel.
credit: Front Face of a Stela (Free-standing Stone with Relief), 692. Mesoamerica, Guatemala, Department of the Petén, El Perú (also known as Waka'), Maya (250-900), Classic period (200-1000). Limestone; overall: 274.4 x 182.3 cm (108 1/16 x 71 3/4 in.)

title: Terpsichore Lyran (Muse of Lyric Poetry)
reveal_text: Trained by his grandfather, an Italian stonemason, Canova began modeling clay and carving marble sculptures before age ten.
credit: Terpsichore Lyran (Muse of Lyric Poetry), 1816. Antonio Canova (Italian, 1757–1822). Marble; overall: 177.5 x 78.1 x 61 cm (69 7/8 x 30 3/4 x 24 in.)

title: Standing Buddha
reveal_text: Around the time when this sculpture was made, portable bronzes began to replace relics as the principal sacred commodity circulating in the Buddhist world.
credit: Standing Buddha, 591. Northern India, Uttar Pradesh. Bronze; overall: 46.5 x 15.4 x 13.4 cm (18 5/16 x 6 1/16 x 5 1/4 in.); without base: 35 x 13.8 x 10.5 cm (13 3/4 x 5 7/16 x 4 1/8 in.)

title: Madonna Nursing the Christ Child
medium: pen and brown ink and brush and brown wash over red and black chalk, with traces of white; framing lines in pen and brown ink
reveal_text: The inscription Morillo on this drawing is not the artist's signature but rather a notation likely made by a 17th-century owner of the drawing.
credit: Madonna Nursing the Christ Child, c. 1670–72. Bartolomé Esteban Murillo (Spanish, 1617–1682). Pen and brown ink and brush and brown wash over red and black chalk, with traces of white; framing lines in pen and brown ink; sheet: 21.4 x 15.4 cm (8 7/16 x 6 1/16 in.); secondary support: 24.1 x 18.5 cm (9 1/2 x 7 5/16 in.)

title: Repository for the Great Perfection of Wisdom Sutra and Eleven Volumes of the Great Perfection of Wisdom Sutra
medium: lacquered wood with ink, color, gold, cut gold, and metalwork
reveal_text: The mate to this object is in the Nara National Museum in Japan.
credit: Repository for the Great Perfection of Wisdom Sutra and Eleven Volumes of the Great Perfection of Wisdom Sutra (大般若経厨子及び大般若経内11巻), late 1100s. Japan, Heian period (794–1185). Lacquered wood with ink, color, gold, cut gold, and metalwork; height: 160 cm (63 in.)

title: Mother Goddess
reveal_text: This sculpture was one of twelve that were brought to a modern temple known as the Tanesar (Thaneshvara) Mahadeva Temple.
credit: Mother Goddess, c. 600. Northwestern India, Rajasthan, Udaipur District. Schist; overall: 79.4 x 23.2 cm (31 1/4 x 9 1/8 in.)

title: Green Tara
medium: thangka; gum tempera, ink, and gold on sized cotton; mount: silk
artist_display: Aniko (Nepalese, active in Tibet mid- to late 1200s)
reveal_text: The tiny monastic figure under Green Tara's right hand commissioned the painting.
credit: Green Tara, c. 1260s, mounted 2020. Attributed to Aniko (Nepalese, active in Tibet mid- to late 1200s). Thangka; gum tempera, ink, and gold on sized cotton; mount: silk; painting: 52.4 x 43.2 cm (20 5/8 x 17 in.); overall: 107 x 65 cm (42 1/8 x 25 9/16 in.)

title: Portrait of Hottō Enmyō Kokushi
medium: Hinoki cypress wood with lacquer, metal staples and fittings
reveal_text: Two famous sculptural portraits of this monk, one at the temple Ankokuji in Hiroshima, and another at the temple Kōkokuji in Wakayama, are dated to 1275 and 1286, respectively.
credit: Portrait of Hottō Enmyō Kokushi (法燈円明国師像), c. 1295–1315. Japan, Kamakura period (1185–1333). Hinoki cypress wood with lacquer, metal staples and fittings; overall: 91.4 cm (36 in.)

title: Danaë
reveal_text: The gauzy scarf draped over Danaë was a studio prop present in many of Orazio's paintings.
credit: Danaë, c. 1623. Orazio Gentileschi (Italian, 1563–1639). Oil on canvas; framed: 202.5 x 270 x 9 cm (79 3/4 x 106 5/16 x 3 9/16 in.); unframed: 162 x 228.5 cm (63 3/4 x 89 15/16 in.)

title: Funerary Portrait of a Young Girl
reveal_text: This painting can be dated based on the hairstyle popular at the end of the reign of Emperor Tiberius (reigned 14–37 CE).
credit: Funerary Portrait of a Young Girl, c. 25–37 CE. Egypt, Roman Empire, late Tiberian. Encaustic on linden wood; overall: 39.4 x 17.4 cm (15 1/2 x 6 7/8 in.)
department: Egyptian and Ancient Near Eastern Art

title: Vase with Golden Pheasants
medium: porcelain painted in polychrome enamels over transparent glaze
culture_display: China, Jiangxi province, Jingdezhen kilns, Qing dynasty (1644-1911), Qianlong mark and period (1736-95)
reveal_text: Refined porcelains produced in Jingdezhen kilns were sent to Beijing palace workshops to be decorated in falangcai technique under direct imperial supervision.
credit: Vase with Golden Pheasants (珐琅彩錦雞花卉蒜頭瓶), 1736–95. China, Jiangxi province, Jingdezhen kilns, Qing dynasty (1644-1911), Qianlong mark and period (1736-95). Porcelain painted in polychrome enamels over transparent glaze; overall: 18.6 x 10.5 cm (7 5/16 x 4 1/8 in.)

title: Priest-King or Deity
medium: basalt with bone eyes (left, ancient; right, restored)
reveal_text: The left eye of the sculpture is the original bone inlay while the right eye is a modern restoration done after the piece was damaged.
credit: Priest-King or Deity, c. 1600 BCE. Hittite, North Syria. Basalt with bone eyes (left, ancient; right, restored); overall: 87.6 cm (34 1/2 in.)
department: Egyptian and Ancient Near Eastern Art

title: Black-Figure Dinos (Mixing Vessel): Warships (Int.); Heroic Scenes (Top)
artist_display: Antimenes Painter (Greek, Attic, active c. 530–510 BCE)
reveal_text: Unable to stand on its own, this round-bottomed vessel probably once had a separately made base.
credit: Black-Figure Dinos (Mixing Vessel): Warships (Int.); Heroic Scenes (Top), c. 520–515 BCE. Circle of Antimenes Painter (Greek, Attic, active c. 530–510 BCE). Ceramic; diameter: 50.8 cm (20 in.); overall: 33.6 cm (13 1/4 in.); diameter of rim: 34 cm (13 3/8 in.)

title: Buddha
culture_display: Thailand, probably Shri Thep, Mon-Dvaravati style
reveal_text: Now reunited for display, CMA acquired the sculpture in two pieces, when the feet (CMA 1974.91) and the body were found in separate locations.
credit: Buddha, 600s. Thailand, probably Shri Thep, Mon-Dvaravati style. Sandstone; overall: 132.7 cm (52 1/4 in.); without tang: 114.2 cm (44 15/16 in.)

title: Storage Jar
medium: Stoneware with natural ash glaze (Shigaraki ware)
reveal_text: This jar was probably made using a coiling method, where coils of clay are stacked atop each other and smoothed to form its structure.
credit: Storage Jar (壺), late 1300s–1400s. Japan, Muromachi period (1392–1573). Stoneware with natural ash glaze (Shigaraki ware); height: 45.7 cm (18 in.); diameter: 42 cm (16 9/16 in.)

title: Ballgame Palma
culture_display: Mexico, Gulf Coast, Classic Veracruz style (600-1100)
reveal_text: The Mesoamerican ballgame was played with a hard rubber ball that players bounced off their hips.
credit: Ballgame Palma, c. 900–1100. Mexico, Gulf Coast, Classic Veracruz style (600-1100). Stone; overall: 49.2 x 23.5 x 11.4 cm (19 3/8 x 9 1/4 x 4 1/2 in.)

title: Seated Female Nude (Self-Portrait?)
reveal_text: Paula Modersohn-Becker's career was extremely brief but prolific before dying from complications of childbirth at age 31.
credit: Seated Female Nude (Self-Portrait?), c. 1899. Paula Modersohn-Becker (German, 1876–1907). Charcoal with stumping; sheet: 62.2 x 33.9 cm (24 1/2 x 13 3/8 in.)

title: Mirror with Phoenixes, Birds, Butterflies, and Floral Sprays
medium: bronze with silver and gold inlaid lacquer
reveal_text: Mirrors like this have a polished side for viewing and a decorated side with a central knob for lifting.
credit: Mirror with Phoenixes, Birds, Butterflies, and Floral Sprays (金銀平脫花鳥鏡), 700s. China, Tang dynasty (618-907). Bronze with silver and gold inlaid lacquer; diameter: 19.2 cm (7 9/16 in.)

title: Seated Buddha
reveal_text: A 1906 photo taken at Kōfukuji shows this sculpture without a halo or left hand.
credit: Seated Buddha (如来坐像), 1100s. Japan, Heian period (794–1185). Gilded wood; overall: 145.4 cm (57 1/4 in.); figure: 88.2 cm (34 3/4 in.); pedestal: 57.2 cm (22 1/2 in.)

title: Black-Figure Hydria (Water Vessel): Frontal Quadriga (Body); Theseus and Minotaur (Shoulder)
artist_display: Antimenes Painter (Greek, Attic, active c. 530–510 BCE)
reveal_text: This type of water vessel, a hydria, takes its name from hudor, Greek for water (and still familiar in the English root hydro).
credit: Black-Figure Hydria (Water Vessel): Frontal Quadriga (Body); Theseus and Minotaur (Shoulder), c. 520 BCE. Attributed to Antimenes Painter (Greek, Attic, active c. 530–510 BCE). Ceramic; overall: 43.2 cm (17 in.); diameter of rim: 24.7 cm (9 3/4 in.); diameter of foot: 15.2 cm (6 in.)

title: The Birth and Naming of John the Baptist
reveal_text: Flemish artists are famed for their attention to minute detail. Here the artist paints a domestic setting from his own time period, complete with details like a brazier for heating in the lower right corner and concave mirror above the bed.
credit: The Birth and Naming of John the Baptist, 1496–99. Juan de Flandes (Netherlandish, c. 1460–1519). Oil on wood; framed: 99 x 60 x 7.5 cm (39 x 23 5/8 x 2 15/16 in.); unframed: 88.4 x 49.9 cm (34 13/16 x 19 5/8 in.)

title: The Crucifixion of Saint Andrew
reveal_text: Although Saint Andrew is typically associated with an X-shaped cross, here he is depicted on a standard vertical cross.
credit: The Crucifixion of Saint Andrew, 1606–7. Caravaggio (Italian, 1571–1610). Oil on canvas; framed: 233.5 x 184 x 12 cm (91 15/16 x 72 7/16 x 4 3/4 in.); unframed: 202.5 x 152.7 cm (79 3/4 x 60 1/8 in.)

title: Niagara Falls by Moonlight
reveal_text: In this drawing, Church manipulates negative space—the space around and between his drawn subjects—to suggest the forms of water and smoke.
credit: Niagara Falls by Moonlight, probably 1856. Frederic Edwin Church (American, 1826–1900). Graphite and white gouache; sheet: 11.6 x 16.4 cm (4 9/16 x 6 7/16 in.)

title: Nome Gods Bearing Offerings
culture_display: Egypt, New Kingdom (1540–1069 BCE), Dynasty 18, reign of Amenhotep III (1390–1352 BCE)
reveal_text: Another block from the same temple wall was acquired 15 years earlier by the museum.
credit: Nome Gods Bearing Offerings, c. 1391–1353 BCE. Egypt, New Kingdom (1540–1069 BCE), Dynasty 18, reign of Amenhotep III (1390–1352 BCE). Painted limestone; overall: 66 x 133 cm (26 x 52 3/8 in.)
department: Egyptian and Ancient Near Eastern Art

title: Covered Tureen on Stand (Pot-à-oille)
artist_display: Juste-Aurèle Meissonnier (French, 1695–1750); Pierre-François Bonnestrenne (French); Henry Adnet (French, 1745)
reveal_text: The langoustine, pigeon, and vegetables were cast using real examples giving the silver copies lifelike detail and appearance.
credit: Covered Tureen on Stand (Pot-à-oille), 1735–38. Juste-Aurèle Meissonnier (French, 1695–1750), Pierre-François Bonnestrenne (French), Henry Adnet (French, 1745). Silver; overall: 36.9 x 38.4 x 31.8 cm (14 1/2 x 15 1/8 x 12 1/2 in.); average: 35 x 38.4 x 31.8 cm (13 3/4 x 15 1/8 x 12 1/2 in.)

title: Corner Railing Pillar with Drinking Scenes, Yakshis, and Musicians
reveal_text: The celestial musicians, who play a lyre, castanets, and a triangular harp, evoke bacchanalian connotations.
credit: Corner Railing Pillar with Drinking Scenes, Yakshis, and Musicians, 100s CE. India, Mathura, Kushan period. Red sandstone; overall: 80 x 22.9 x 24 cm (31 1/2 x 9 x 9 7/16 in.)

title: Church Street El
reveal_text: Sheeler and his good friend, the poet William Carlos Williams, patronized speakeasies together during Prohibition.
credit: Church Street El, 1920. Charles Sheeler (American, 1883–1965). Oil on canvas; framed: 60 x 67.5 x 6 cm (23 5/8 x 26 9/16 x 2 3/8 in.); unframed: 41 x 48.5 cm (16 1/8 x 19 1/8 in.)

title: Raft Cup
medium: hammered silver soldered together, with chased decoration
culture_display: China, Yuan (1271-1368) or early Ming dynasty (1368-1644)
reveal_text: The traditional understanding of the silver vessel 's function is that it was supposed to hold wine.
credit: Raft Cup (銀槎杯), 1300s–1400s. Attributed to Zhu Bishan (Chinese, c. 1300–after 1362). Hammered silver soldered together, with chased decoration; overall: 16 cm (6 5/16 in.)

title: Studies of a Seated Female, Child's Head, and Three Studies of a Baby
reveal_text: This sheet was probably once part of a sketchbook carried by the artist Raphael on a 1508 journey between Florence and Rome.
credit: Studies of a Seated Female, Child's Head, and Three Studies of a Baby, c. 1507–8. Raphael (Italian, 1483–1520). Metalpoint; sheet: 12 x 15.3 cm (4 3/4 x 6 in.)

title: François Tronchin
reveal_text: Jean-Etienne Liotard created portraits of eight other members of the Tronchin family around the same time as this pastel.
credit: François Tronchin, 1757. Jean-Etienne Liotard (Swiss, 1702–1789). Pastel on parchment; unframed: 38 x 46.3 cm (14 15/16 x 18 1/4 in.)

title: Red-Figure Lekythos (Oil Vessel): Athena Slaying Giant (body); Satyr between Maenads (shoulder)
artist_display: Berlin Painter (Greek, Attic, active c. 505–460 BC); Painter of Goluchow 37 (Greek, Attic, active c. 490–460 BC)
reveal_text: First attributed to Douris, this vase is now thought to have been painted by two anonymous painters.
credit: Red-Figure Lekythos (Oil Vessel): Athena Slaying Giant (body); Satyr between Maenads (shoulder), c. 490 BCE. Shoulder attributed to Berlin Painter (Greek, Attic, active c. 505–460 BC), body attributed to Painter of Goluchow 37 (Greek, Attic, active c. 490–460 BC). Ceramic; diameter: 14.1 cm (5 9/16 in.); diameter of mouth: 8.7 cm (3 7/16 in.); overall: 38 cm (14 15/16 in.); diameter of foot: 10 cm (3 15/16 in.)

title: Head of King Userkaf
culture_display: Egypt, Old Kingdom (2647–2124 BCE), Dynasty 5, reign of Userkaf (2496–2491 BCE)
reveal_text: Visible chisel marks on the side of the crown imply that another figure once stood to the king's right; only a god or goddess could have stood in this prominent position.
credit: Head of King Userkaf, c. 2454–2447 BCE. Egypt, Old Kingdom (2647–2124 BCE), Dynasty 5, reign of Userkaf (2496–2491 BCE). Painted limestone; overall: 6.5 x 7.2 cm (2 9/16 x 2 13/16 in.); face: 4.8 x 4.8 cm (1 7/8 x 1 7/8 in.)
department: Egyptian and Ancient Near Eastern Art

title: Arhat
medium: Hanging scroll; ink and slight color on silk
reveal_text: This painting that depicts an arhat, a human being who has achieved enlightenment, used to be part of a set of 500 hanging scrolls.
credit: Arhat (세공양존자 [世供養尊者]), 1235. Korea, Goryeo dynasty (918–1392). Hanging scroll; ink and slight color on silk; image: 52.7 x 40.6 cm (20 3/4 x 16 in.); overall: 140 x 54.1 cm (55 1/8 x 21 5/16 in.)

title: Portrait of Ōzora Buzaemon
medium: hanging scroll; ink and color on paper
reveal_text: Watanabe Kazan was interested in Rangaku (Dutch Learning).
credit: Portrait of Ōzora Buzaemon (大空武左衛門像), 1827. Watanabe Kazan (Japanese, 1793–1841). Hanging scroll; ink and color on paper; image: 221.8 x 117.8 cm (87 5/16 x 46 3/8 in.); overall: 256.8 x 131 cm (101 1/8 x 51 9/16 in.); with knobs: 256.8 x 140.5 cm (101 1/8 x 55 5/16 in.)

title: Dancing Sailors
reveal_text: This drawing was exhibited only once during the artist's lifetime, at the Museum of Modern Art in New York—notable since he otherwise did not show them publicly.
credit: Dancing Sailors, 1917. Charles Demuth (American, 1883–1935). Watercolor over graphite; sheet: 20.4 x 25.7 cm (8 1/16 x 10 1/8 in.); secondary support: 21.4 x 26.3 cm (8 7/16 x 10 3/8 in.)

title: The Holy Family on the Steps
reveal_text: Poussin placed small wax models in a box pierced with holes to study lighting effects.
credit: The Holy Family on the Steps, 1648. Nicolas Poussin (French, 1594–1665). Oil on canvas; framed: 103.5 x 135.3 x 13.3 cm (40 3/4 x 53 1/4 x 5 1/4 in.); unframed: 73.3 x 105.8 cm (28 7/8 x 41 5/8 in.); former: 72.3 x 104 cm (28 7/16 x 40 15/16 in.)

title: Hide Shirt
medium: hide, pigment, glass beads, human hair
culture_display: America, Native North American, Central Plains, Lakota Sioux, 19th century
reveal_text: Only Lakota (Sioux) chiefs had the right to wear painted shirts, an honor they earned through bravery.
credit: Hide Shirt, c. 1890. America, Native North American, Central Plains, Lakota Sioux, 19th century. Hide, pigment, glass beads, human hair; overall: 97.8 x 150 cm (38 1/2 x 59 1/16 in.)

title: The Biglin Brothers Turning the Stake
reveal_text: Rowing was among the most popular spectator sports in the U.S. during the 1870s.
credit: The Biglin Brothers Turning the Stake, 1873. Thomas Eakins (American, 1844–1916). Oil on canvas; framed: 117 x 167 x 6.5 cm (46 1/16 x 65 3/4 x 2 9/16 in.); unframed: 101.3 x 151.4 cm (39 7/8 x 59 5/8 in.)

title: Figure of a Warrior
culture_display: Central Mexico, Tetzcoco?, Aztec, Post-Classic Period
reveal_text: The calendar glyphs on the figure's back read "2 Rabbit, 3 Water."
credit: Figure of a Warrior, after 1325 (thermoluminescence date of casting core, 1345–1575). Central Mexico, Tetzcoco?, Aztec, Post-Classic Period. Gold-silver-copper alloy (cast); overall: 11.2 x 6.1 cm (4 7/16 x 2 3/8 in.)

title: Hen and Chicks Covered Tureen on Stand
reveal_text: Though the form of a soup tureen suggests a functional role at the dining table, such large, expensive porcelains were probably only used for decoration because hot liquids might have easily caused them to crack.
credit: Hen and Chicks Covered Tureen on Stand, c. 1755. Chelsea Porcelain Factory (Britain, London, 1745–84). Soft-paste porcelain; overall: 24.8 x 34.9 x 25.7 cm (9 3/4 x 13 3/4 x 10 1/8 in.)

title: Fire-flame Cooking Vessel (Ka'en Doki)
medium: Earthenware with carved and applied decoration
reveal_text: Scientifically tested residues from Jōmon vessels reveal that their makers consumed detoxified acorns.
credit: Fire-flame Cooking Vessel (Ka'en Doki) (火焔土器), c. 2500 BCE. Japan, Jōmon period (c. 10,500–300 BCE). Earthenware with carved and applied decoration; height: 61 cm (24 in.); diameter: 55.8 cm (21 15/16 in.)

title: Mask (mbap mteng): Elephant (aka)
medium: Cotton, burlap, glass beads, twine, leather, and wood
culture_display: Africa, Central Africa, Cameroon, Grassfields region, Bamileke-style maker
reveal_text: Performers wore elephant masks with indigo-dyed robes, red feather headdresses, and leopard pelts. Leopards and elephants symbolized royal power.
credit: Mask (mbap mteng): Elephant (aka), early 1900s. Africa, Central Africa, Cameroon, Grassfields region, Bamileke-style maker. Cotton, burlap, glass beads, twine, leather, and wood; overall: 139.7 x 50.8 x 19.1 cm (55 x 20 x 7 1/2 in.)

title: Basin with Inscribed Figures and Calligraphy
reveal_text: A child, one of the main decorative elements for this basin, is possibly a young Buddhist pilgrim named Sudhana, who took a long spiritual journey to search for enlightenment.
credit: Basin with Inscribed Figures and Calligraphy (청동 은입사 호 [靑銅銀入絲兩耳壺]), 1100s–1200s. Korea, Goryeo dynasty (918–1392). Bronze; overall: 17 cm (6 11/16 in.); diameter of mouth with rim: 21.7 cm (8 9/16 in.)

title: Trophy of the Hunt
reveal_text: Adolphe Braun’s hunt still lifes continue a tradition popular in Northern European painting from the 17th century on.
credit: Trophy of the Hunt, c. 1867. Adolphe Braun (French, 1812–1877). Carbon print; image: 78 x 59.8 cm (30 11/16 x 23 9/16 in.); framed: 99.7 x 79.4 cm (39 1/4 x 31 1/4 in.)

title: Hell Courtesan
medium: Hanging scroll; ink, color, gold, and silver on silk
reveal_text: The word for longevity appears multiple times in gold and silver on her outer robe.
credit: Hell Courtesan (地獄太夫図), 1871–89. Kawanabe Kyōsai (Japanese, 1831–1889). Hanging scroll; ink, color, gold, and silver on silk; image: 144.2 x 67.6 cm (56 3/4 x 26 5/8 in.); overall: 233.7 x 92.1 cm (92 x 36 1/4 in.)

title: Sideboard
reveal_text: Luscious fruit, wild animals, and Native American figures are masterfully carved into this walnut sideboard. These decorative adornments were created for a distinctively American taste and would have symbolized abundance and wealth to visitors who encountered it within a grand dining room.
credit: Sideboard, c. 1855. Attributed to Joseph Alexis Bailly (American, 1825–1883). Walnut; overall: 290.4 x 211.4 x 69.4 cm (114 5/16 x 83 1/4 x 27 5/16 in.)

title: Winter Landscape
medium: hanging scroll; ink and slight color on silk
reveal_text: This wintry scenery was mainly painted in monochrome ink, but some details like the rider's horse saddle and the servant's carrier attest the use of light blue pigment.
credit: Winter Landscape (설경산수도 [雪景山水圖]), 1500s. Yeoseol (Korean). Hanging scroll; ink and slight color on silk; image: 92.4 x 52.7 cm (36 3/8 x 20 3/4 in.); overall: 195.6 x 74.9 cm (77 x 29 1/2 in.)

title: Jar with Dragon and Clouds Design
reveal_text: A mythical creature believed to have the power to make rain, the dragon is a prominent symbol of rulers in premodern East Asia; the image of dragons was used exclusively for the king's paraphernalia.
credit: Jar with Dragon and Clouds Design (철화 백자 운룡무늬 호 [鐵畵白磁雲龍文壺]), late 1600s. Korea, Joseon dynasty (1392–1910). Porcelain with underglaze iron; diameter of base: 13.7 cm (5 3/8 in.); overall: 34.7 cm (13 11/16 in.)

title: Jar with Dragon Design
reveal_text: A mythical creature believed to have the power to make rain, the dragon is a prominent symbol of rulers in premodern East Asia; the image of dragons was used exclusively for the king's paraphernalia.
credit: Jar with Dragon Design (백자 청화 운룡무늬 호 [白磁靑畵雲龍文壺]), 1700s. Korea, Joseon dynasty (1392–1910). Porcelain with underglaze blue; diameter of base: 14.7 cm (5 13/16 in.); overall: 39.5 cm (15 9/16 in.)

title: Snowscape with Figures
reveal_text: Having retreated to the snow-covered mountains, a scholar sits in his studio surrounded by trees. An empty wooden bridge in the distance appears to be longing for friends from afar. Such iconography often evokes the idea of an idealized recluse focusing on scholarly pursuits.
