credit: Snowscape with Figures (한림제설도 [寒林霽雪圖]), 1584. Kim Si (Korean, 1524–1593). Hanging scroll; ink on silk; painting only: 53 x 67.3 cm (20 7/8 x 26 1/2 in.); overall: 148.6 x 90.2 cm (58 1/2 x 35 1/2 in.)

title: Buddhist Priest's Ceremonial Robe
reveal_text: The Five Transcendent Buddhas, who symbolize the purity of the five elements, are repeated around the edges.
credit: Buddhist Priest's Ceremonial Robe (袈裟), 1400s. China, Ming dynasty (1368–1644). Silk and gold thread; embroidery; overall: 119.4 x 302.1 cm (47 x 118 15/16 in.)

title: Rustam's seventh course: He kills the White Div, folio 124 from a Shah-nama (Book of Kings) of Firdausi (Persian, about 934–1020)
medium: Gum tempera, ink, gold, and silver on paper
artist_display: Mir Musavvir (Iranian, c. 1510–1555); Abd al-Vahhab (Persian, active c. 1516)
reveal_text: The Shahnameh is a mixture of a mythology and a history of the Iranian people.
credit: Rustam's seventh course: He kills the White Div, folio 124 from a Shah-nama (Book of Kings) of Firdausi (Persian, about 934–1020), 1520–37. Attributed to Mir Musavvir (Iranian, c. 1510–1555), attributed to Abd al-Vahhab (Persian, active c. 1516). Gum tempera, ink, gold, and silver on paper; sheet: 47.5 x 32.2 cm (18 11/16 x 12 11/16 in.); image: 28.4 x 18.5 cm (11 3/16 x 7 5/16 in.)

title: Bell (Lai Zhong)
culture_display: China, Shaanxi province, Meixian, Western Zhou dynasty (c. 1046–771 BCE)
reveal_text: The technical sophistication of the unusual lens-shaped cavity allowed the bell to produce two different tones.
credit: Bell (Lai Zhong) (逨鐘), c. 800–700 BCE. China, Shaanxi province, Meixian, Western Zhou dynasty (c. 1046–771 BCE). Bronze; overall: 70.3 x 37 x 26.6 cm (27 11/16 x 14 9/16 x 10 1/2 in.)

title: Seven Jeweled Mountain
medium: ten-panel screen; ink and color on silk
reveal_text: Featuring a spectacular panoramic view and topographical details, this ten-panel folding screen is an excellent 19th-century version of the Korean “true-view” landscape painting tradition.
credit: Seven Jeweled Mountain (칠보산도 [七寶山圖]), late 1800s. Korea, Joseon dynasty (1392–1910). Ten-panel screen; ink and color on silk; overall: 185.2 x 42.9 cm (72 15/16 x 16 7/8 in.); each screen: 158.2 x 42.9 cm (62 5/16 x 16 7/8 in.)

title: Memorial head (nsodie)
culture_display: Ghana, Akan-style sculpture, unknown female ceramicist
reveal_text: The woman who sculpted this head did so without making a sketch, working from her memory of the subject.
credit: Memorial head (nsodie), late 1600s–early 1700s. Ghana, Akan-style sculpture, unknown female ceramicist. Terracotta; overall: 19.1 x 13.6 x 15.5 cm (7 1/2 x 5 3/8 x 6 1/8 in.)

title: The Power of Music
reveal_text: Mount was an accomplished fiddle player and even patented a new design for the instrument.
credit: The Power of Music, 1847. William Sidney Mount (American, 1807–1868). Oil on canvas; framed: 67 x 78 x 7.5 cm (26 3/8 x 30 11/16 x 2 15/16 in.); unframed: 43.4 x 53.5 cm (17 1/16 x 21 1/16 in.)

title: "Horned" Male
medium: earthenware with colored slips, black burial deposits
reveal_text: This figure’s “horn” may be the spire of a conch shell, a reference to fertility.
credit: "Horned" Male, 100 BCE–300 CE. West Mexico, Colima, Comala style. Earthenware with colored slips, black burial deposits; overall: 41.5 x 24.9 x 24.7 cm (16 5/16 x 9 13/16 x 9 3/4 in.)

title: Tea Table
medium: inlaid wood (mahogany?), cast and gilded bronze mounts, inlays of ivory or bone, metal, and mother-of-pearl (marine mussels or pearl oysters)
reveal_text: Carlo Bugatti's sons achieved illustrious careers of their own. Rembrandt Bugatti (1884-1916) became a celebrated sculptor, and Ettore Bugatti (1881-1947) manufactured luxurious automobiles and racing cars.
credit: Tea Table, c. 1907. Carlo Bugatti (Italian, 1856–1940). Inlaid wood (mahogany?), cast and gilded bronze mounts, inlays of ivory or bone, metal, and mother-of-pearl (marine mussels or pearl oysters); overall: 71.5 x 67.1 x 41.3 cm (28 1/8 x 26 7/16 x 16 1/4 in.)

title: Standing Officer Holding a Boar's Spear
medium: Pen and brown ink, brush and brown wash, red chalk with traces of white opaque watercolor over black chalk, on paper; traces of transfer
reveal_text: This military officer brandishes a type of spear used for boar hunting, in which the two lugs on the blade were meant to keep the animal from pushing its way up the spear to attack the hunter.
credit: Standing Officer Holding a Boar's Spear, 1586. Hendrick Goltzius (Dutch, 1558–1617). Pen and brown ink, brush and brown wash, red chalk with traces of white opaque watercolor over black chalk, on paper; traces of transfer; sheet: 20.6 x 15.6 cm (8 1/8 x 6 1/8 in.)

title: Avatamsaka Sutra No. 78
medium: Folded book with illustrated frontispiece; gold and silver on mulberry paper
reveal_text: The pristine condition of this manuscript indicates that it was rarely used after its creation, revealing that the act of making a manuscript rather than reading it was the main religious goal.
credit: Avatamsaka Sutra No. 78 (대방광불화엄경 권제78 [大方廣佛華嚴經 卷第七十八]), 1300s. Korea, Goryeo dynasty (918–1392). Folded book with illustrated frontispiece; gold and silver on mulberry paper; book: 20.6 x 43.7 cm (8 1/8 x 17 3/16 in.); each page: 20.7 x 11 cm (8 1/8 x 4 5/16 in.)

title: Cosmetic Jar in the Form of the God Bes
reveal_text: Bes was the Egyptian god of many things, including cosmetics, war, and childbirth.
credit: Cosmetic Jar in the Form of the God Bes, 664–525 BCE. Egypt, Late period (715–332 BCE), Dynasty 26. Egyptian blue; overall: 9.3 x 6.1 x 4.1 cm (3 11/16 x 2 3/8 x 1 5/8 in.)
department: Egyptian and Ancient Near Eastern Art

title: Young Woman (possibly a Sex Worker) with a Man (recto); Young Woman Wringing Her Hands over a Man's Naked Body (verso)
medium: brush and gray and black washes on laid paper
reveal_text: Much of Francisco de Goya's graphic output of drawings and prints was not made public until well after his death.
credit: Madrid Album "B": Young Woman (possibly a Sex Worker) with a Man (recto); Young Woman Wringing Her Hands over a Man's Naked Body (verso), 1796–97. Francisco de Goya (Spanish, 1746–1828). Brush and gray and black washes on laid paper; image: 18.7 x 12 cm (7 3/8 x 4 3/4 in.); sheet: 23.5 x 14.5 cm (9 1/4 x 5 11/16 in.)

title: Head
culture_display: Africa, West Africa, Nigeria, Nok-culture style region, unknown female ceramicist
reveal_text: This complete head’s bold modeling, deliberate asymmetry, and sensitive expression make it one of the finest known examples of Nok sculpture.
credit: Head, thermoluminescence date 20–620 CE. Africa, West Africa, Nigeria, Nok-culture style region, unknown female ceramicist. Terracotta; overall: 38.2 x 20 cm (15 1/16 x 7 7/8 in.)

title: Crucifix with Scenes of the Passion
reveal_text: Although the artist did sign this panel at the bottom, the signature is illegible, his first name perhaps being Michael.
credit: Crucifix with Scenes of the Passion, c. 1230–40. Italy, Pisa. Tempera with gold on panel; framed: 186.6 x 160.7 x 12.7 cm (73 7/16 x 63 1/4 x 5 in.); unframed: 185 x 160 x 10.2 cm (72 13/16 x 63 x 4 in.)

title: Lidded Bowl with Iguana
reveal_text: Green iguanas can grow to be over 6 feet long.
credit: Lidded Bowl with Iguana, c. 600–1100 (Thermoluminescence date, 995–1395). Costa Rica, Southern Nicoya region. Ceramic, slip; base: 15.1 x 35 cm (5 15/16 x 13 3/4 in.); lid: 29.9 x 38 cm (11 3/4 x 14 15/16 in.)

title: Half Armor for the Foot Tournament
medium: etched and gilded steel, brass rivets, leather and velvet fittings
reveal_text: Pompeo della Cesa was the Armani or Gucci of the 1500s; everyone wanted to wear him.
credit: Half Armor for the Foot Tournament, c. 1590. Pompeo della Cesa (Italian, active 1572–93). Etched and gilded steel, brass rivets, leather and velvet fittings; overall: 31.7 x 27.7 x 21.6 cm (12 1/2 x 10 7/8 x 8 1/2 in.)

title: Prestige Cap (mpu ngola)
medium: Raffia palm fiber (Raphia ruffia or R. vinifera) and leopard claws
culture_display: Africa, Central Africa, Democratic Republic of Congo, Republic of Congo, or Cabinda (Angola), Kongo-style maker, possibly Yombe subgroup
reveal_text: The geometric motifs on this mpu cap—including diamonds and interlaces—are seen on Kongo, Vili, and Yombe works in other materials, including ivory and basketry.
credit: Prestige Cap (mpu ngola), late 1800s–early 1900s. Africa, Central Africa, Democratic Republic of Congo, Republic of Congo, or Cabinda (Angola), Kongo-style maker, possibly Yombe subgroup. Raffia palm fiber (Raphia ruffia or R. vinifera) and leopard claws; overall: 42 x 19.3 x 19 cm (16 9/16 x 7 5/8 x 7 1/2 in.)

title: Fire Screen
medium: gilded wood, painted and gilded wood panels, brocaded silk, embossed paper
reveal_text: Produced by the New York decorating firm Herter Brothers, this screen would have been used to shield sitters from the heat of a fireplace.
credit: Fire Screen, c. 1878–80. Firm of Herter Brothers (American). Gilded wood, painted and gilded wood panels, brocaded silk, embossed paper; overall: 131.8 x 76.2 x 58.3 cm (51 7/8 x 30 x 22 15/16 in.)

title: Operating on Guan Yu's Arm
medium: hanging scroll; ink, color, and gold on silk
reveal_text: This is the largest surviving painting by the artist.
credit: Operating on Guan Yu's Arm (関羽割臂図), 1840s. Katsushika Ōi (Japanese, c. 1800-after 1857). Hanging scroll; ink, color, and gold on silk; mounted: 206.7 x 73.1 cm (81 3/8 x 28 3/4 in.); painting: 140.2 x 68.3 cm (55 3/16 x 26 7/8 in.)

title: Portrait of Tieleman Roosterman
reveal_text: Roosterman may have ordered this portrait--and one of his wife--to commemorate their wedding in 1631.
credit: Portrait of Tieleman Roosterman, 1634. Frans Hals (Dutch, c. 1581–1666). Oil on canvas; framed: 139 x 109 x 5.5 cm (54 3/4 x 42 15/16 x 2 3/16 in.); unframed: 117 x 87 cm (46 1/16 x 34 1/4 in.)

title: Tomb Guardian with Animal Head
culture_display: China, probably Shaanxi province, Xi'an, Tang dynasty (618-907)
reveal_text: Sancai (three-color) glazes in green, amber, and transparent white, plus expensive cobalt blue glaze, show the high social status of the tomb’s occupant.
credit: Tomb Guardian with Animal Head (鎮墓獸), early 700s. China, probably Shaanxi province, Xi'an, Tang dynasty (618-907). Glazed earthenware, sancai (three-color) ware; overall: 92.3 x 43.8 x 41.9 cm (36 5/16 x 17 1/4 x 16 1/2 in.)

title: Funeral for Marie-Thérèse of Spain, Dauphine of France, in the Church of Nôtre Dame, Paris, on November 24, 1746
medium: Pen and black ink and gray wash heightened with white gouache on cream laid paper, incised (with graphite) for transfer
reveal_text: Charles-Nicolas Cochin worked closely with the branch of the royal household charged with organizing and presenting royal ceremonies to produce prints such as the one related to this drawing.
credit: Funeral for Marie-Thérèse of Spain, Dauphine of France, in the Church of Nôtre Dame, Paris, on November 24, 1746, c. 1746. Charles-Nicolas Cochin (French, 1715–1790). Pen and black ink and gray wash heightened with white gouache on cream laid paper, incised (with graphite) for transfer; sheet: 45 x 30.9 cm (17 11/16 x 12 3/16 in.)

title: Statuette of Herakles
reveal_text: This robust figure exudes power, despite his damaged feet and missing hands.
credit: Statuette of Herakles, 510–500 BCE. Greek. Bronze (solid cast); overall: 21.5 cm (8 7/16 in.)

title: Sand Dunes, Carson Desert, Nevada
medium: albumen print from wet collodion negative
reveal_text: O’Sullivan took this while on a government expedition analyzing the future path of the transcontinental railroad.
credit: Sand Dunes, Carson Desert, Nevada, 1867. Timothy H. O'Sullivan (American, 1840–1882). Albumen print from wet collodion negative; image: 19.7 x 27 cm (7 3/4 x 10 5/8 in.); mounted: 45.7 x 59.4 cm (18 x 23 3/8 in.); matted: 50.8 x 61 cm (20 x 24 in.)

title: Female Worshiper
culture_display: Crete, Minoan, middle Minoan III–late Minoan I
reveal_text: The shaved head with prominent locks of hair indicates that this female votive figure is an adolescent.
credit: Female Worshiper, c. 1600–1500 BCE. Crete, Minoan, middle Minoan III–late Minoan I. Bronze; overall: 14 cm (5 1/2 in.)

title: Gamin
reveal_text: A trailblazer, Savage was the first African American member of the National Association of Women Artists.
credit: Gamin, c. 1929. Augusta Savage (American, 1892–1962). Hand-painted plaster; overall: 44.5 x 24.2 x 20.4 cm (17 1/2 x 9 1/2 x 8 1/16 in.)

title: The Cathedral
reveal_text: This etching was Ensor’s most popular, and he eventually issued it in two versions; the Cleveland Museum of Art's rare impression is among the first that he printed.
credit: The Cathedral, 1886. James Ensor (Belgian, 1860–1949). Etching; sheet: 31 x 22.4 cm (12 3/16 x 8 13/16 in.); platemark: 24.2 x 18.8 cm (9 1/2 x 7 3/8 in.)

title: The Cleveland Apollo: Apollo Sauroktonos (Lizard-Slayer) or Apollo the Python-Slayer
reveal_text: The Cleveland Apollo is the only surviving large-scale bronze sculpture of its type, Apollo Sauroktonos (the Lizard-Slayer).
credit: The Cleveland Apollo: Apollo Sauroktonos (Lizard-Slayer) or Apollo the Python-Slayer, c. 350–200 BCE. Attributed to Praxiteles (Greek, Athenian, c. 400–330 BCE), or Follower. Bronze, copper, and stone inlay; base: 0.5 x 47.3 x 40.7 cm (3/16 x 18 5/8 x 16 in.); overall: 150 x 50.3 x 66.8 cm (59 1/16 x 19 13/16 x 26 5/16 in.)

title: Staff of Office
culture_display: Central Africa, Democratic Republic of the Congo, Luba
reveal_text: The Yeke kingdom ruler Chief Msidi gave this as a gift of appreciation to the British missionary Frederick Stanley Arnot, whom the chief had invited to his kingdom.
credit: Staff of Office, early to mid-1800s. Central Africa, Democratic Republic of the Congo, Luba. Wood and iron; overall: 170 x 13.3 cm (66 15/16 x 5 1/4 in.)

title: Self-Portrait
reveal_text: This early self-portrait is especially rare among Edgar Degas's prints, and few impressions are known to exist.
credit: Self-Portrait, 1857. Edgar Degas (French, 1834–1917). Etching and drypoint; platemark: 23 x 14.5 cm (9 1/16 x 5 11/16 in.); sheet: 31.5 x 22.6 cm (12 3/8 x 8 7/8 in.)

title: Big-Eared Brown Bat Vessel
culture_display: Central Andes, North Coast, Moche people
reveal_text: This vessel depicts a big-eared brown bat.
credit: Big-Eared Brown Bat Vessel, 200–850 CE. Central Andes, North Coast, Moche people. Ceramic, slip; overall: 18.4 x 17.7 x 15.8 cm (7 1/4 x 6 15/16 x 6 1/4 in.)

title: Winter Trees Reflected in a Pond
medium: salted paper print from calotype negative
reveal_text: Talbot made this image just a few years after he invented the photograph on paper.
credit: Winter Trees Reflected in a Pond, 1841–42. William Henry Fox Talbot (British, 1800–1877). Salted paper print from calotype negative; image: 16.4 x 19.1 cm (6 7/16 x 7 1/2 in.); paper: 19.8 x 24.8 cm (7 13/16 x 9 3/4 in.); matted: 40.6 x 50.8 cm (16 x 20 in.)

title: Brahma-Shiva
culture_display: South India, Tamil Nadu, Chola dynasty, late 10th - early 11th century
reveal_text: The back of the sculpture retains the original textured surface from a pointed chisel, suggesting it was in a niche or near a wall, protected from worshippers’ touches.
credit: Brahma-Shiva, 900s. South India, Tamil Nadu, Chola dynasty, late 10th - early 11th century. Granite; overall: 162.6 x 48 cm (64 x 18 7/8 in.)

title: Frogs and Lily Pads (Grenouilles et Nénuphars) Vase
reveal_text: René Lalique placed little sheets of metal foil behind the applied green frogs to reflect a dark green tint back to the viewer.
credit: Frogs and Lily Pads (Grenouilles et Nénuphars) Vase, 1909–12. René Lalique (French, 1860–1945). Glass; overall: 21 x 29.8 cm (8 1/4 x 11 3/4 in.)

title: Sacrificer Container
reveal_text: Traces of cinnabar, a toxic mercuric sulfide, are visible on the container's surface.
credit: Sacrificer Container, 770–890 (radiocarbon date, 95% probability). Central Andes, Wari style (600-1000). Wood and cinnabar; overall: 10.8 x 7 x 7.5 cm (4 1/4 x 2 3/4 x 2 15/16 in.)

title: The Devil Speaks (Mahna No Varua Ino) (recto); Women Washing Clothes (verso)
reveal_text: In 1921, Paul Gauguin's son Pola printed a new edition of his father's Noa Noa prints, wiping the woodblocks cleanly so the images were more legible.
credit: Noa Noa: The Devil Speaks (Mahna No Varua Ino) (recto); Women Washing Clothes (verso), 1893–1894. Paul Gauguin (French, 1848–1903). Woodcut; sheet: 20.5 x 32.1 cm (8 1/16 x 12 5/8 in.); image: 20.3 x 31.9 cm (8 x 12 9/16 in.)

title: Nursemaids’ Promenade, Frieze of Carriages
medium: color lithographs on wove paper, screen with four panels
reveal_text: Although this screen was issued in an edition of 110, only about 20 copies survive today.
credit: Nursemaids’ Promenade, Frieze of Carriages, 1895. Pierre Bonnard (French, 1867–1947). Color lithographs on wove paper, screen with four panels; sheet: 137.4 x 47.3 cm (54 1/8 x 18 5/8 in.); image: 137.4 x 47.3 cm (54 1/8 x 18 5/8 in.)

title: Julius Caesar
medium: marble with traces of bole (red clay) and limestone with traces of paint
artist_display: Mino da Fiesole (Italian, c. 1430–1484); Mino da Fiesole and Workshop (Italian, c. 1430–1484)
reveal_text: Traces of paint on this white marble relief indicated that it was once brightly painted.
credit: Julius Caesar, c. 1455–60. Mino da Fiesole (Italian, c. 1430–1484), garland by Mino da Fiesole and Workshop (Italian, c. 1430–1484). Marble with traces of bole (red clay) and limestone with traces of paint; overall: 83 x 84 x 25 cm (32 11/16 x 33 1/16 x 9 13/16 in.)

title: The Rabbit Hunt
reveal_text: Pieter Bruegel the Elder's paintings, drawings, and prints often drew from popular proverbs, translating them into pictorial form. In the case of this etching, "A hare yourself, you hunt for prey" is perhaps the most apt.
credit: The Rabbit Hunt, 1560. Pieter Bruegel (Netherlandish, c. 1525/30–1569). Etching; platemark: 22.1 x 29 cm (8 11/16 x 11 7/16 in.); sheet: 24.3 x 31.8 cm (9 9/16 x 12 1/2 in.)

title: Icon of the Mother of God and Infant Christ (Virgin Eleousa)
culture_display: Republic of Venice, Kingdom of Candia, Candia (Heraklion, Crete, Greece) or Byzantine Empire, Constantinople (Istanbul, Turkey)
reveal_text: This is the only known work by Angelos Akotantos in the United States.
credit: Icon of the Mother of God and Infant Christ (Virgin Eleousa), c. 1425–50. Attributed to Angelos Akotantos (Cretan, died 1450). Tempera and gold on wood; unframed: 96 x 70 cm (37 13/16 x 27 9/16 in.)

title: Pair of Candelabra
medium: cut and polished steel with gold and silvered decoration
reveal_text: Decorative works in steel like this pair of candelabra were made by gun makers in the town of Tula, Russia, to impress the empress, Catherine the Great.
credit: Pair of Candelabra, c. 1790–95. Russia, Tula. Cut and polished steel with gold and silvered decoration; overall: 40.7 x 24.8 cm (16 x 9 3/4 in.)

title: Male Figure (nkishi)
medium: Wood, glass beads, brass (including upholstery studs), copper alloy, iron alloy, raffia, reedbuck antelope horn, rawhide, animal hair, human teeth, organic material, minerals, and plant fibers
culture_display: Africa, Central Africa, Democratic Republic of Congo, Songye-style carver and nganga (priest)
reveal_text: Researchers conducted X-ray scanning on this figure, revealing its internal structure and human teeth, an antelope horn, and animal hair.
credit: Male Figure (nkishi), late 1800s–early 1900s. Africa, Central Africa, Democratic Republic of Congo, Songye-style carver and nganga (priest). Wood, glass beads, brass (including upholstery studs), copper alloy, iron alloy, raffia, reedbuck antelope horn, rawhide, animal hair, human teeth, organic material, minerals, and plant fibers; overall: 64 x 24.5 x 24 cm (25 3/16 x 9 5/8 x 9 7/16 in.)

title: Indian Combat
reveal_text: Growing up in her Indigenous community, Lewis went by a name that translates as "Wildfire."
credit: Indian Combat, 1868. Edmonia Lewis (American, c. 1844–1907). Marble; overall: 76.2 x 48.3 x 36.5 cm (30 x 19 x 14 3/8 in.)

title: Books and Scholars’ Accoutrements
medium: ten-panel folding screen; ink and color on silk
reveal_text: A convincing hypothesis that connects the Korean chaekgado tradition with the Renaissance tradition of illusionistic studiolo through the technique of trompe-l'œil and Chinese Qing period treasured cabinet paintings was first made in Park Shim-eun's thesis (2002).
credit: Books and Scholars’ Accoutrements (책가도 [冊架圖]), late 1800s. Yi Taek-gyun (Korean, 1808–after 1883). Ten-panel folding screen; ink and color on silk; overall: 197.5 x 395 cm (77 3/4 x 155 1/2 in.); painting only: 139.3 x 330.8 cm (54 13/16 x 130 1/4 in.)

title: Desk and Bookcase
medium: "Plum pudding" mahogany, red cedar, chestnut, white pine, brass
reveal_text: This desk and bookcase was likely made for Oliver Wolcott Sr., a signer of the Declaration of Independence and later governor of Connecticut.
credit: Desk and Bookcase, c. 1780–95. Attributed to John Townsend (American, 1732–1809). "Plum pudding" mahogany, red cedar, chestnut, white pine, brass; overall: 240 x 108 x 64.8 cm (94 1/2 x 42 1/2 x 25 1/2 in.)

title: Monsieur Boileau at the Café
medium: oil and tempera with charcoal on millboard
reveal_text: The green drink on the table is probably absinthe, a popular drink with artists at the time that was thought to have hallucinogenic properties.
credit: Monsieur Boileau at the Café, 1893. Henri de Toulouse-Lautrec (French, 1864–1901). Oil and tempera with charcoal on millboard; sheet: 80.3 x 65 cm (31 5/8 x 25 9/16 in.); framed: 105.4 x 89.5 x 8.3 cm (41 1/2 x 35 1/4 x 3 1/4 in.)

title: Writing Box (Suzuribako) with Phoenix in Paulownia
medium: Lacquer on wood with sprinkled gold and silver powder (maki-e) and gold and silver foil application
reveal_text: In contrast to the bright, celebratory motifs on the exterior, the interior of the box invites a more reflective mood with its autumn moonlit sky over chrysanthemums.
credit: Writing Box (Suzuribako) with Phoenix in Paulownia (桐に鳳凰金銀蒔絵硯箱), late 1500s. Japan, Momoyama period (1573–1615). Lacquer on wood with sprinkled gold and silver powder (maki-e) and gold and silver foil application; 4 x 20.5 x 23.5 cm (1 9/16 x 8 1/16 x 9 1/4 in.)

title: Saint John the Baptist
reveal_text: St. John the Baptist holds a book and a lamb, referencing his description of Christ as "the lamb of God."
credit: Saint John the Baptist, c. 1500. Attributed to Jan Crocq (Netherlandish, act. 1486–1510). Tonnerre limestone; overall: 163 x 59 x 40 cm (64 3/16 x 23 1/4 x 15 3/4 in.)

title: Pair of Candle Stands (torchères)
reveal_text: Candle stands usually held silver or crystal candelabra that were lit for evening entertainment.
credit: Pair of Candle Stands (torchères), c. 1773. Thomas Chippendale (British, 1718–1779). Gilt-wood, gesso; each: 154 x 56 x 51 cm (60 5/8 x 22 1/16 x 20 1/16 in.)

title: Dish with Ginkgo Leaves
medium: Porcelain with underglaze blue (Hizen ware, Nabeshima type)
reveal_text: The ginkgo's distinctive fan-shaped leaf is a symbol of longevity and endurance in Japanese art.
credit: Dish with Ginkgo Leaves (染付銀杏唐花文皿), late 1600s–early 1700s. Japan, Edo period (1615–1868). Porcelain with underglaze blue (Hizen ware, Nabeshima type); diameter: 20 cm (7 7/8 in.)

title: Figure 45: Contraction électrique forte des triangulaires des lèvres et des sourciliers: douleur et déspoir
medium: Albumen print from glass plate negative
reveal_text: Duchenne, a neurologist at a hospital for the poor in Paris, turned to photography to record the grammar of human expression.
credit: Mécanisme de la physionomie humaine: Figure 45: Contraction électrique forte des triangulaires des lèvres et des sourciliers: douleur et déspoir, c. 1856, printed 1862. Guillaume-Benjamin-Amand Duchenne (de Boulogne) (French, 1806–1875), Adrien Tournachon (French, 1825–1903). Albumen print from glass plate negative; image: 23.5 x 17.9 cm (9 1/4 x 7 1/16 in.); oval opening: 16.2 x 11.9 cm (6 3/8 x 4 11/16 in.); mounted: 41.2 x 27.4 cm (16 1/4 x 10 13/16 in.)

title: The Fourth King of Hell
medium: Hanging scroll; ink, color, and gold ink on silk
reveal_text: As the concept of salvation became increasingly more emphasized in Buddhist schools in medieval East Asia, the gruesome depiction of various hells was given an important role: to promote Buddhist ethics. In the Buddhist infernal otherworld, the Ten Kings of Hell serve as judges of the deceased to determine their fates, including the type and severity of punishment.
credit: The Fourth King of Hell, late 1300s. Korea, Goryeo dynasty (918–1392). Hanging scroll; ink, color, and gold ink on silk; image: 63 x 45.3 cm (24 13/16 x 17 13/16 in.); overall: 136.2 x 58.9 cm (53 5/8 x 23 3/16 in.)

title: Tomb with Sphinxes and an Owl
reveal_text: The growing popularity of Egypt in the late 18th century, bolstered by Napoleon Bonaparte’s ultimately failed attempt to colonize the country, ensured a ready market for the subject of Louis Jean Desprez's print.
credit: Tomb with Sphinxes and an Owl, c. 1779–84. Louis Jean Desprez (French, 1743–1804). Aquatint with etching; platemark: 34 x 48.7 cm (13 3/8 x 19 3/16 in.); sheet: 35 x 50.3 cm (13 3/4 x 19 13/16 in.)

title: Still Life with Meat, Fish, Vegetables, and Fruit
medium: Oil on panel, the reverse prepared with gesso
reveal_text: Hulsdonck thoughtfully included condiments here: butter, lemon, parsley for the trotters, and a dab of mustard for the ham.
credit: Still Life with Meat, Fish, Vegetables, and Fruit, c. 1615–20. Jacob van Hulsdonck (Flemish, 1582–1647). Oil on panel, the reverse prepared with gesso; 71.5 x 104 cm (28 1/8 x 40 15/16 in.)

title: Sleeping Christ Child
reveal_text: Tender images of the Christ child sleeping encouraged devout Christians to meditate on Christ’s humanity.
credit: Sleeping Christ Child, c. 1675. Filippo Parodi (Italian, 1630–1702). Marble; 63 x 102 x 42 cm (24 13/16 x 40 3/16 x 16 9/16 in.)

title: At the Café
reveal_text: This image appeared in the Belgian journal L’Europe, which was censored by Parisian police. This impression is the only one known to have been saved before the journal was destroyed.
credit: At the Café, 1874. Édouard Manet (French, 1832–1883). Gillotage; image: 26.3 x 33.4 cm (10 3/8 x 13 1/8 in.); sheet: 26.9 x 36 cm (10 9/16 x 14 3/16 in.)

title: Knife Sheath
reveal_text: This 12th-century silver-gilt knife sheath is meant to be a pendent to a belt as a luxury ornament.
credit: Knife Sheath (장도집), 1100s. Korea, Goryeo dynasty (918–1392). Gilt silver (body) and brass (trefoil-shaped ring); length: 21.7 cm (8 9/16 in.)

title: The Dutton Family in the Drawing Room of Sherborne Park, Gloucestershire
reveal_text: This is an idealized, not actual, room showing the sitters’ fashionable interior decorating aspirations.
credit: The Dutton Family in the Drawing Room of Sherborne Park, Gloucestershire, c. 1772. Johann Zoffany (German, 1733–1810). Oil on canvas; unframed: 101.5 x 127 cm (39 15/16 x 50 in.); framed: 124.5 x 149.9 x 7.6 cm (49 x 59 x 3 in.)

title: Nautilus Cup
artist_display: Cornelis Jansz van der Burch (Dutch, active in Delft, d. 1615)
reveal_text: The nautilus is a marine mollusk that swims by expelling water from its shell using jet propulsion.
credit: Nautilus Cup, 1607. Cornelis Jansz van der Burch (Dutch, active in Delft, d. 1615). Silver-gilt, nautilus shell; overall: 33.3 x 10.6 x 20.1 cm (13 1/8 x 4 3/16 x 7 15/16 in.)

title: Flower Pyramid
artist_display: Adrianus Kocx (Dutch, active 1686–1701); De Grieksche A (The Greek A) Factory (Netherlands, 1658–1811)
reveal_text: The iconic blue-and-white ceramics from Delft originated as an attempt to copy Chinese porcelain.
credit: Flower Pyramid, c. 1690. Adrianus Kocx (Dutch, active 1686–1701), De Grieksche A (The Greek A) Factory (Netherlands, 1658–1811). Tin-glazed earthenware, painted in blue; overall: 95 cm (37 3/8 in.)

title: Fata Morgana
reveal_text: The sculpture originally appeared to emerge mysteriously from a cave within a constructed grotto.
credit: Fata Morgana, c. 1572. Giambologna (Flemish, active Italy, 1529–1608). Marble; overall: 99 x 68 cm (39 x 26 3/4 in.)

title: Standing Ksitigarbha
medium: Hanging scroll; ink, color, and gold on silk
reveal_text: Only 10 of the 14th-century paintings of Ksitigarbha are known to exist in the world today. This is one of these rare surviving masterpieces.
credit: Standing Ksitigarbha (지장보살), 1300–1350. Korea, Goryeo dynasty (918–1392). Hanging scroll; ink, color, and gold on silk; painting: 102.6 x 43.2 cm (40 3/8 x 17 in.)

title: Foliate-Shaped Tray with Mother-of-Pearl Inlay
medium: Inlaid abalone shells on black lacquer ground, applied with red lacquer on the interior
reveal_text: This tray’s intricately decorated surface was created through a labor-intensive inlay technique employing thin, precisely cut abalone shell.
credit: Foliate-Shaped Tray with Mother-of-Pearl Inlay, late 1400s–early 1500s. Korea, Joseon dynasty (1392–1910). Inlaid abalone shells on black lacquer ground, applied with red lacquer on the interior; width: 44.1 cm (17 3/8 in.)

title: Grapevine
medium: eight-panel folding screen; ink on paper
artist_display: Choe Seok-hwan (Korean, active first half of 19th century)
reveal_text: Grapes in premodern East Asia symbolize many children.
credit: Grapevine (묵포도도 [墨葡萄圖]), early 1800s. Choe Seok-hwan (Korean, active first half of 19th century). Eight-panel folding screen; ink on paper; image: 100.5 x 345 cm (39 9/16 x 135 13/16 in.)

title: Orchids and Rocks
medium: Eight-panel folding screen; ink on silk
reveal_text: The artist of this elegant painting is the formidable politician Yi Ha-eung (1820–1898), who is known for a seclusion policy that aimed to protect Korea and its sovereignty from foreign influences.
credit: Orchids and Rocks (난석도 [蘭石圖]), 1897–98. Yi Ha-eung (Korean, 1820–1898). Eight-panel folding screen; ink on silk; overall: 185 x 52.6 cm (72 13/16 x 20 11/16 in.); each panel: 111.9 x 1.5 cm (44 1/16 x 9/16 in.)

title: Mountain Market, Clear with Rising Mist
reveal_text: Due to its stylistic features, this painting has long been believed to be a Chinese painting of the Northern Song period, dated to the 12th century.
credit: Mountain Market, Clear with Rising Mist (산시청람도 [山市晴嵐圖]), early 1500s. Korea, Joseon dynasty (1392–1910). Hanging scroll; ink on silk; painting: 115.3 x 60 cm (45 3/8 x 23 5/8 in.); overall: 179 x 70.7 cm (70 1/2 x 27 13/16 in.); overall with knobs and cord: 181.8 x 77 cm (71 9/16 x 30 5/16 in.)

title: Stag at Sharkey's
reveal_text: George Bellows was raised in Columbus, Ohio, and attended Ohio State University where he played baseball and basketball.
credit: Stag at Sharkey's, 1909. George Bellows (American, 1882–1925). Oil on canvas; framed: 110 x 140.5 x 8.5 cm (43 5/16 x 55 5/16 x 3 3/8 in.); unframed: 92 x 122.6 cm (36 1/4 x 48 1/4 in.)

title: Coffin of Bakenmut
culture_display: Egypt, Thebes, Third Intermediate (1069–715 BCE), Dynasties 21–22
reveal_text: Originally another smaller coffin was placed inside this outer coffin and in that the deceased with a mummy board would have rested.
credit: Coffin of Bakenmut, c. 1000–900 BCE. Egypt, Thebes, Third Intermediate (1069–715 BCE), Dynasties 21–22. Gessoed and painted sycamore fig; overall: 208 x 68 cm (81 7/8 x 26 3/4 in.)
department: Egyptian and Ancient Near Eastern Art

title: Coffin of Nesykhonsu
culture_display: Egypt, Thebes, Third Intermediate (1069–715 BCE), Dynasties 21–22
reveal_text: Two of Nesykhonsu's titles, "Lady of the House" and "Singer of the Choir of Mut the Great," are written on the outside of the coffin.
credit: Coffin of Nesykhonsu, c. 976–889 BCE. Egypt, Thebes, Third Intermediate (1069–715 BCE), Dynasties 21–22. Gessoed and painted sycamore fig; overall: 70 cm (27 9/16 in.)
department: Egyptian and Ancient Near Eastern Art

title: Lamellophone (chisanji)
culture_display: Africa, Central Africa, Democratic Republic of the Congo or Angola, Chokwe-style maker
reveal_text: The CMA acquired this plucky instrument in 1915, one year before it opened to the public.
credit: Lamellophone (chisanji), late 1800s. Africa, Central Africa, Democratic Republic of the Congo or Angola, Chokwe-style maker. Wood, iron, and wire; overall: 19.1 x 10.2 x 6.4 cm (7 1/2 x 4 x 2 1/2 in.)

title: Dancers
medium: pastel with charcoal on tracing paper mounted on paper and backed with gray board
reveal_text: Degas joined six separate irregularly shaped sheets of paper to make this work, and their connections are faintly visible on its surface.
credit: Dancers, c. 1896. Edgar Degas (French, 1834–1917). Pastel with charcoal on tracing paper mounted on paper and backed with gray board; sheet: 55.7 x 41.4 cm (21 15/16 x 16 5/16 in.)

title: The Age of Bronze
reveal_text: The sculpture suggests the heroism and suffering of his countrymen during the Franco-Prussian War of 1870–71. This life-sized male figure was modeled after a Belgium soldier named August Neyt.
credit: The Age of Bronze, 1875–76. Auguste Rodin (French, 1840–1917). Bronze; with base: 182.2 x 66.4 x 47 cm (71 3/4 x 26 1/8 x 18 1/2 in.)

title: Amitabha Triad
reveal_text: The small scale suggests that this rare bronze triad might have been displayed in a personal shrine rather than a large worship hall.
credit: Amitabha Triad (금동아미타여래삼존좌상 [金銅阿彌陀如來三尊坐像]), 1400s. Korea, Joseon dynasty (1392–1910). Bronze with traces of gilding; overall: 40.6 x 16.5 x 54.6 cm (16 x 6 1/2 x 21 1/2 in.)

title: Bridal Robe (Hwarot)
medium: Satin weave silk; silk embroidery; paper edging on neck and sleeves
reveal_text: This bride's robe was not made for one specific bride, but rather was shared and passed down to many brides.
